data.description:
Problem statement
-----------------
Add mxbai-embed-large-v1 as a dedicated, small embeddings model that the proxy can route to when callers request model `embeddings`. The proxy should resolve the alias, route requests to a local llama-server hosting the model, and return results in the OpenAI embeddings response format.

Users
-----
- Primary users: downstream services and frontend components that POST to `/v1/embeddings` to obtain text embeddings (example: the Web UI embeddings test and server-side features that index or search text).
- Secondary users: engineers who need a low-cost, local embeddings model for development and testing.

User stories
- As a frontend developer, when I call POST /v1/embeddings with `model: "embeddings"`, I receive a valid OpenAI-style embeddings response containing a non-empty vector of the expected dimension.
- As an operator, I can run a local llama-server serving `mxbai-embed-large-v1` and the proxy will route embedding requests to it.

Success criteria
----------------
- POST /v1/embeddings with `model: "embeddings"` returns a valid OpenAI embeddings response (id, object, data with vector) and non-empty vector values.
- The proxy resolves the alias `embeddings` → `mxbai-embed-large-v1` and routes requests to the configured local llama-server via existing `proxy_to_local` flow.
- Integration test: an automated test posts sample input and validates response shape and that the returned vector length matches the model's documented embedding dimension.
- The change does not break existing endpoints (models, completions, chat) and existing tests continue to pass.

Constraints
-----------
- Hosting: model will be served by a local llama-server instance (per stakeholder guidance).
- Memory: model is small and may be loaded alongside other models in memory; avoid large memory allocations or global state that prevents co-residency.
- API compatibility: responses must follow the OpenAI embeddings format (non-streaming).
- Do not change behavior for other model names; resolution must be additive (alias only).

Existing state
--------------
- Current work item: LP-0MN557XBD0H8B8PC — title: "Add an embeddings specific model". Description: "Add mxbai-embed-large-v1 as an embeddings model to the proxy... It should respond to requests for a model called 'embeddings'".
- The proxy already implements a dedicated `/v1/embeddings` endpoint and routing logic. See `proxy/server.py` which contains a `@app.post('/v1/embeddings')` handler and calls to `proxy_to_local` / `proxy_to_remote`.
- Related work in `proxy/` added the embeddings endpoint and verification tasks (see related work below). The proxy has a README entry referencing `POST /v1/embeddings`.

Desired change
--------------
- Add model configuration and aliasing so `embeddings` maps to `mxbai-embed-large-v1`.
- Ensure model config marks `mxbai-embed-large-v1` as supporting embeddings and routed to the local llama-server backend.
- Add/instrument integration tests that POST to `/v1/embeddings` with `model: "embeddings"` (and omitted model case if desired) and validate the OpenAI-format response and vector dimension.
- Document deployment/runtime expectations for the local llama-server hosting the model.

Related work
------------
- proxy/server.py — contains `/v1/embeddings` endpoint and routing logic; commits and comments reference adding the endpoint and validation. (proxy/server.py)
- proxy/README.md — documents Embeddings endpoint usage. (proxy/README.md)
- Work items: "Implement dedicated /v1/embeddings endpoint handler" (LM-0MN23BEY91TZ38Y7), "Verify existing tests pass and embeddings endpoint works" (LM-0MN23BLWZ1LJHEDJ), and parent feature "Support embeddings via proxy" (LM-0MN239W8C1BTXTB5).

Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers
--------------------------------------
- Q: "Where should the small embedding model be hosted/served? (pick one or provide a short freeform answer)"
  — Answer (user): "Local llama-server (use existing proxy_to_local)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "How should requests be resolved when model='embeddings' or when model is omitted?"
  — Answer (user): "Alias 'embeddings' → 'mxbai-embed-large-v1' (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "Which of these acceptance criteria should we include (pick one or provide freeform additions)?"
  — Answer (user): "Functional + integration tests (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

Notes and open questions
------------------------
- OPEN QUESTION: Should the proxy treat an omitted `model` field on `/v1/embeddings` as equivalent to `model: "embeddings"`, or require explicit model selection? (user selected alias behavior; confirm default-on-omission behavior.)

data.description:
## Summary
Document the step-by-step process and checklist for adding a new model (including embeddings models) to the proxy so engineers can implement and configure models consistently.

## Scope
- Describe required code changes (model config entries, aliasing, routing).
- Describe deployment/runtime expectations (local llama-server packaging, memory constraints).
- List tests to add (integration test for /v1/embeddings, shape/dimension checks, regression tests).
- Provide sample config snippets and a worked example adding  as .

## Implementation checklist
1) Add example model config entry in the proxy models config (fields: name, alias, supports: [embeddings], backend: local).
2) Show how to add alias resolution (map 'embeddings' -> 'mxbai-embed-large-v1').
3) Document how to ensure routing uses  for local backends and how to register the model in any model registry used by the proxy.
4) Add an automated integration test that POSTs to /v1/embeddings with model: 'embeddings' and validates OpenAI embeddings format and vector dimension.
5) Add runtime notes for running the local llama-server with the model, memory expectations, and example docker/launch commands.
6) Add a short troubleshooting section (common errors, how to verify model loaded).

## Acceptance criteria
- A new doc (docs/add-model.md or proxy/MODEL_ADD.md) is created and committed describing the full process with examples.
- The repo contains an integration test demonstrating  alias works and returns OpenAI-format embeddings.
- The doc references parent work item LP-0MN557XBD0H8B8PC and is linked from the parent description.
data.title: Document process for adding a new model to the proxy

data.description:
Problem statement
-----------------
Add an expanded statistics panel under the main status bar that surfaces key runtime and model metrics for the currently loaded model (model name, llama-server process status, max context (n_ctx), KV cache token count, and cumulative tokens sent/received). The proxy will query the llama-server over HTTP for model metadata and use existing SSE broadcasts to push snapshots to clients in real time.

Users
-----
- Operators / SREs monitoring model health and usage.
- Frontend developers and product engineers who test and debug local models.
- Automated tests/CI and developers who rely on observable diagnostic metrics.

User stories
-----------
- As an operator, I can see the currently loaded model and whether the llama-server is Running, Stopped, or Switching in the top-level status area.
- As a developer, I can open the expanded stats panel and read the model's max context (n_ctx) and current KV cache tokens to verify context usage during tests.
- As a tester, the UI receives SSE updates and cumulative token counters increase after requests, enabling automated checks.

Success criteria
----------------
- The main page shows an expanded stats panel under the status bar with labeled values for: Model name, llama-server status (Running/Stopped/Switching), Max context (n_ctx), KV cache token count, Total tokens sent, Total tokens received.
- Values update in real time via the existing SSE/status broadcasts; SSE messages include the new fields.
- Server-side: proxy queries llama-server status endpoints for n_ctx and KV cache values and includes them, plus cumulative `token_counts` (total_sent, total_recv), in SSE snapshots.
- Tests: Playwright tests assert the panel renders, SSE payloads include the keys, and token counters increase after a test request.
- No regressions to existing endpoints or tests.

Constraints
-----------
- Data source: n_ctx and KV cache counts are obtained by querying llama-server HTTP endpoints (see Desired change). If the backend does not include the fields, the proxy will return `unknown` for those values and the UI will show a tooltip explaining the backend limitation.
- Update method: SSE is the primary delivery mechanism; fallback/robustness around SSE connection handling is required but polling is not the main path.
- Token counters: display cumulative totals since process start (use existing `token_counts` keys `total_sent` / `total_recv`).
- Security: do not broadcast or display prompts, API keys, secrets, or user content.

Existing state
--------------
- `proxy/server.py` implements SSE helpers (`broadcast_status`, `broadcast_status_sync`, `_periodic_broadcast_loop`) and maintains `token_counts` and `request_counts` state. The index page is rendered server-side in the `index` handler; tests exist under `proxy/tests` for status bar behaviour.
- Existing Playwright tests exercise SSE and status elements: `proxy/tests/status-bar.spec.js`.

Desired change
--------------
Server-side
- Implement a small helper `query_llama_status()` in `proxy/server.py` that:
  - Attempts HTTP GET to `http://localhost:<llama_port>/model` then `/status` and returns parsed JSON if successful.
  - If those endpoints are not present or do not include `n_ctx` / KV cache values, return `null` for those fields.
  - Integrate this helper into the existing `_periodic_broadcast_loop()` so the SSE snapshot includes: `current_model`, `llama_server_running`, `n_ctx` (or null), `kv_cache_tokens` (or null), and cumulative `token_counts` entries `total_sent` and `total_recv`.
- Add defensive parsing and logging; do not block broadcasts if the backend is unreachable — include `llama_server_running=false` and `n_ctx=null` in that case.

Client-side (UI)
- Add an expanded stats panel directly beneath the status bar. The panel shows labeled rows: `Model`, `Llama-server status`, `Max context`, `KV cache tokens`, `Total tokens sent`, `Total tokens received`.
- Wire the existing SSE handler to update these fields live. For missing values show `unknown` with a hover tooltip: "Value not available from backend".
- Keep the panel lightweight: only update DOM nodes whose values changed.

Tests & QA
- Playwright tests: extend or add `proxy/tests/status-stats.spec.js` with full checks: load `/`, attach SSE listener, verify SSE payload contains `n_ctx` and `kv_cache_tokens` keys (or null), assert panel renders, and after posting a test request assert `total_sent`/`total_recv` increase.
- Unit tests: add unit tests for the `query_llama_status()` helper (simulate endpoints returning full/partial/missing fields) and for SSE payload assembly.

Related work
------------
- `proxy/server.py` — SSE, token counting, broadcasting, and index page rendering. (proxy/server.py)
- `proxy/tests/status-bar.spec.js` — existing Playwright tests to extend. (proxy/tests/status-bar.spec.js)
- `proxy/MODEL_ADD.md` — model configuration guidance. (proxy/MODEL_ADD.md)
- Related work item: LP-0MN557XBD0H8B8PC — Add an embeddings specific model (related but separate). (LP-0MN557XBD0H8B8PC)

Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers
--------------------------------------
- Q: "Which model/runtime statistics should appear on the main page status area? Pick the ones you want (multiple OK). Suggested default: Max context, KV cache used, model name, llama-server status." — Answer (user): "Max context (llama.n_ctx()) (Recommended), KV cache token count (llama.get_kv_cache_token_count()), Total tokens sent/received (proxy counters), Model name (current_model), llama-server process status (Running/Stopped/Switching)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "How should these stats be presented in the UI? Pick one (or freeform). Recommended: compact badges in the existing status bar so it remains lightweight." — Answer (user): "Expanded stats panel under the status bar". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "How should the stats update? Pick one. Recommended: use existing SSE/status broadcasts for live updates where possible." — Answer (user): "Real-time via SSE/status broadcasts (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "How should the UI obtain max context (n_ctx) and KV cache token count? (pick one or describe). Recommended: proxy queries llama-server status endpoint and proxy broadcasts them via SSE." — Answer (user): "Proxy queries llama-server status endpoint and includes values in SSE (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "If the llama-server status endpoint does not return n_ctx or KV cache counts, how should the UI behave?" — Answer (user): "Show the row with value `unknown` and a tooltip explaining the backend limitation (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "Which llama-server endpoint should the proxy query for n_ctx and KV cache counts?" — Answer (user): "Can we use the python code in the issue description?" — Clarified and confirmed: use the existing `proxy/server.py` implementation and add a `query_llama_status()` helper that attempts HTTP GET on `/model` then `/status` (recommended), parsing fields when present; fallback is `null`. Source: interactive reply and confirmation (user selected option 1). Final: yes.
- Q: "What should the Playwright tests assert for this feature?" — Answer (user): "Full checks: panel renders, fields present, SSE payload contains keys, and token counters increase after a test request (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

Notes and open questions
------------------------
- OPEN QUESTION: If different deployments expose fields under different names (e.g., `kv_cache_tokens` vs `kv_cache_token_count`), should the proxy include a small mapping table in config? (Recommended: start with common field names and add a follow-up spike if field name variation appears in environments.)
- RISK: SSE not available to some clients or network conditions—tests should cover SSE fallback and the UI should surface a graceful message if SSE disconnects.

Top-line summary
----------------
Implement HTTP-based queries from `proxy/server.py` to local llama-server endpoints (`/model` → `/status`), include `n_ctx` and KV cache counts in SSE snapshots, and render them in an expanded, SSE-updated stats panel under the main status bar; missing values display as `unknown` with tooltip. Add Playwright and unit tests.

data.description:
Problem statement
-----------------
The proxy index page currently displays the top-left status label "Server Running". This should be updated to "Proxy Running" to more accurately describe the process and avoid ambiguity for users.

Users
-----
- Operators and developers who open the proxy index page to check service status.

User stories
-----------
- As an operator, I want the top-left status label to read "Proxy Running" so it's clear the proxy process (not an upstream server) is running.
- As a developer, I want tests to assert the new label so CI catches regressions.

Success criteria
----------------
- The index page status label displays the exact text `Proxy Running` in place of the previous `Server Running`.
- Repository code and templates are updated in the single source of truth (proxy/server.py) and no other unintended occurrences remain.
- Tests that previously asserted the old label are updated so CI validates the new string and the change is covered by automated checks.

Constraints
-----------
- Keep the change minimal: update only the index page status bar text unless additional occurrences are explicitly requested.
- Do not change the underlying status logic or process semantics — this is a label/text change only.

Existing state
--------------
- The index page HTML is rendered from `proxy/server.py` and contains the status bar markup. The current label is the literal text `Server Running` (line in file updated in commit `51a8adb`).
- There were no other code locations that matched the exact `Server Running` string during a repository search (conservative search results).

Desired change
--------------
- Replace the literal `Server Running` text in `proxy/server.py` index HTML with `Proxy Running`.
- Update any Playwright or UI tests that assert the old text to expect the new `Proxy Running` string.
- Add a short wl comment linking the commit that made the change and record the work item progress.

Related work
------------
- `proxy/server.py` — index page rendering and status bar. (proxy/server.py) — contains the changed label and is the single point of edit.
- Work item: Rename 'Server Started' on the proxy index page to say 'Proxy Started' (LP-0MN5SW5SE1J1GQ5O) — current item being updated.

Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers
--------------------------------------
- Q: "Which exact label should be displayed in the top-left status bar on the proxy index page? (Recommended first option)" — Answer (user): "Replace current text with `Proxy Running`". Source: interactive reply during intake interview. Final: yes.
- Q: "Where should this label update be applied?" — Answer (user): "Only the index page status bar (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply during intake interview. Final: yes.
- Q: "How should we verify the change?" — Answer (user): "Update tests (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply during intake interview. Final: yes.

Change log / verification notes
------------------------------
- Implemented change in `proxy/server.py`: replaced `Server Running` with `Proxy Running`.
- Commit: 51a8adb — message: "LP-0MN5SW5SE1J1GQ5O: Rename status label 'Server Running' → 'Proxy Running' and update intake".
- A wl comment was added to LP-0MN5SW5SE1J1GQ5O recording the commit (comment id: LP-C0MN7OT7Y9001OTJM).

Top-line summary
----------------
Replace the index page status label `Server Running` with `Proxy Running` and update tests to assert the new label so CI protects against regressions.
data.title: Rename 'Server Started' on the proxy index page to say 'Proxy Started'

data.description:
Implement robustness around model startup after reboot.

Goal:
- Ensure llama-server can be started even if distrobox or user runtime is not immediately available after boot.
- Capture and log child process stdout/stderr when spawn fails.
- Retry start attempts with exponential backoff for a short window to tolerate boot-order races.

Acceptance criteria:
-  attempts distrobox, falls back to direct script, and retries for a configurable short window.
- On immediate child exit the proxy logs captured stdout/stderr and broadcasts a helpful error message.
-  includes dependencies on  and  to avoid starting before container runtime is ready.
data.title: Harden startup: systemd unit + start_llama_server retry and stderr capture

data.description:
Build a self-contained container image that includes llama-server and all runtime dependencies necessary to serve models without mounting the host home directory.

Goals and acceptance criteria:
- Create Dockerfile/Containerfile under proxy/container/ that installs llama-server (binary or build steps) and required runtime libs.
- Container exposes the llama-server HTTP port (configurable) and runs as a non-root user inside the container where feasible.
- No host home directory mounts required for model runtime: models either embedded in the image or mounted from a dedicated host directory (e.g., /var/lib/llama-models) mounted read-only by system podman when running the container.
- Provide a build script  to build the image locally and tag it .
- Document image build and run instructions in  or a new .

Suggested implementation approach:
1. Create  that installs system deps and copies Unrecognized model (''). 
Supported models: GPT120, Qwen3, Qwen2.5 and related scripts.
2. Provide an entrypoint that runs the internal  and accepts model name via environment variable or command argument.
3. Add a build script and local run example.

Priority: high
data.title: Containerize llama-server for system podman deployment

data.description:
Remove systemd and service-installation logic from proxy/install.sh. Keep virtualenv creation, dependency installation, log directory guidance, and manual start instructions so the script can be used to start the proxy in development or ad-hoc deployments. Files changed: proxy/install.sh. Acceptance criteria:
- install.sh no longer attempts to install or configure systemd services.
- install.sh still creates/uses the python venv and installs Python dependencies.
- install.sh prints clear manual start instructions.
data.title: Remove systemd/service management from proxy startup script

data.description:
Summary: Remove systemd unit files and associated installation instructions for llama-proxy from the repository.

Details:
- Delete files: proxy/llama-proxy.service, proxy/llama-proxy.user.service
- Update docs and install scripts to remove any instructions that copy or advise installing these service files (proxy/README.md, proxy/install.sh, proxy/HOST_INSTALL.md, proxy/setup-linger-uidmap.sh).

Acceptance criteria:
1) The two .service files are removed from the repo.
2) No README or install script instructs copying these service files to systemd directories.
3) Worklog item includes references to all changed files and commit hash in comments when changes are committed.

Reason: The service should no longer be distributed from the repo; users should run the proxy manually or manage systemd units outside this repo.

data.title: Remove installed systemd unit from host for llama-proxy

data.description:
Improve log output — colour-code severities and improve readability on the Log page

Problem statement
Improve the proxy Log page so log lines are visually distinct by severity (errors, warnings, info, etc.), improving scanability and making it easier for engineers to spot issues at a glance while preserving accessibility.

Users
- Primary: Internal developers and on-call engineers who use the /logs page to investigate proxy behaviour and incidents.
- Secondary: Developers doing development/debugging and automation owners who may review logs during CI runs.

User stories
- As an engineer, I want error lines highlighted so I can find failures quickly when troubleshooting.
- As an on-call, I want warning and info lines visually distinct and labelled so I can triage alerts faster.
- As an accessible user, I want colour to be supplemented with icons and text labels so I can interpret severity without relying on colour alone.

Acceptance Criteria (measurable)
1. The /logs page displays log lines colour-coded by severity (error, warning, info, debug/trace when present) and shows a short severity badge or icon for each line. (Verification: reviewers inspect live page or screenshots; badges present for sample error/warn/info lines.)
2. Colours and badges meet WCAG contrast/accessibility requirements (e.g., contrast ratios validated with an automated/a11y tool); colour is not the only indicator — icons or visible short text labels are present. (Verification: run contrast checks and manual a11y audit.)
3. CSS variables exist for severity colours (e.g., --log-error, --log-warning, --log-info, --log-debug) and default to existing design tokens when available (e.g., fall back to --error, --warning, --text-secondary). (Verification: review CSS and demonstrate fallbacks in devtools.)
4. There is a user-configurable toggle to enable/disable colourized view; the preference persists (e.g., in localStorage or a user setting). (Verification: toggle works and persists across page reloads.)
5. Automated unit tests cover the client-side severity-detection/parsing logic (text heuristics and any structured parsing implemented) and these tests pass. (Verification: added tests run and pass.)
6. Manual validation: a PR includes screenshots and a brief manual QA checklist (desktop + mobile) demonstrating correct rendering for error/warn/info/debug lines. (Verification: PR contains screenshots and checklist.)
7. All related documentation is updated to reflect the change, including code comments, proxy/README.md (logs section), and any relevant wiki/docs site entries. (Verification: docs updated in PR.)
8. Full project test suite must pass with the new changes. (Verification: CI / local test run succeeds.)

Constraints
- Do not change log generation or backend log formats — only the UI rendering on /logs is in scope.
- The UI must not materially increase SSE bandwidth or log tailing latency; per-line decoration should be client-side only.
- Prefer reuse of existing design tokens where feasible; expose new CSS variables that default to those tokens.

Existing state
- The proxy provides a /logs page implemented in proxy/server.py which streams log lines from /logs/tail and renders them inside a <div id="log"> with monospace content. Styles are currently inline in the HTML template and do not apply severity-specific classes.

Desired change
- Client-side parsing of each incoming log line to detect severity using conservative heuristics (leading "ERROR", "WARN", "INFO", "DEBUG") and use structured fields when present.
- Add severity CSS classes and a small severity badge/icon per rendered line.
- Add CSS variables for severity colours with sensible defaults that reference existing tokens.
- Accessibility-first presentation: colour + icon + short label and ensure WCAG contrast. Provide a toggle to disable colourized view.

Related work
- proxy/server.py — Log page template and SSE tail implementation (file: proxy/server.py)
- proxy/README.md — Documentation describing logs and tailing endpoints (file: proxy/README.md)
- Harden startup: systemd unit + ... (LP-0MN5T7QNY18TUTT6) — operational work touching logs and startup behaviour (work item)
- Remove systemd/service management from proxy startup script (LP-0MN6JEIFR034RI4C) — references to log directory guidance (work item)
- Rename 'Server Started' on the proxy index page to say 'Proxy Started' (LP-0MN5SW5SE1J1GQ5O) — example small UI change in repo (work item)

Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers
- Q: "Which screens/components should be updated for the 'Improve log output' work item?" — Answer (user): "Log page (primary)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "Which approach should we use for colour-coding log severities?" — Answer (user): "Semantic palette + icon/text labels". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "Which of the following best describes how we'll evaluate success for this change?" — Answer (user): "Visual change merged + manual QA" (screenshots + manual verification). Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "Which log severities should be colour-coded on the Log page?" — Answer (user): "All severities found in logs (auto-discover)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "Should the change enforce WCAG contrast/accessibility for the colour labels (e.g., add icons/text badges if contrast is low)?" — Answer (user): "Yes — enforce WCAG". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "Should the implementation reuse existing design tokens (theme colors) or add new CSS variables for log severity colours?" — Answer (user): "Both: new variables that default to existing tokens". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

data.description:
Title: Can we load the embed model and another model at the same time?
Work item: LP-0MN7T0KFJ004ALGK

Headline
--------
Investigate and implement support for co-residency of the small embeddings model and one other model so embedding requests do not trigger model switches; prefer dynamic on-demand co-loading in the running llama-server and validate with a concurrent integration test.

Problem statement
-----------------
The proxy currently allows only one model to be loaded in the local llama-server at a time, which forces model switches and can cause latency or 503s when serving embedding requests. The goal is to enable the (small) embeddings model to co-reside with one other model to avoid unnecessary switching for `/v1/embeddings` requests.

Users
-----
- Frontend engineers calling `/v1/embeddings` (example: Web UI embeddings test) — want stable, low-latency embedding responses without triggering model swaps.
- Backend services that index/search text and rely on embeddings as a fast local service.
- Operators running local test/dev environments — want a simple configuration and documented startup steps to host both models.

Example user stories
- As a frontend developer, when I POST /v1/embeddings (model omitted or model: "embeddings") I receive a valid OpenAI-style embeddings response without the proxy switching away from the primary model.
- As an operator, I can run the proxy with the embed model co-resident with a primary model using documented start-up changes (start-llama.sh / container notes).
- As an engineer, I can run an automated test that concurrently issues embedding and non-embedding requests and both succeed.

Success criteria
----------------
- Co-residency: The embeddings model and one other configured model can reside concurrently such that embedding requests do not force a llama-server model switch.
- Compatibility: POST /v1/embeddings with `model: "embeddings"` (and when `model` is omitted if callers omit it) returns a valid OpenAI-style embeddings response (id, object, data with non-empty embedding vector) as before.
- Concurrency test: New integration test that runs 5 concurrent embedding requests and 5 concurrent non-embedding requests while co-residency is active — all requests succeed (HTTP 200) and responses are valid.
- Non-regression: Existing proxy behaviour for other endpoints and models remains unchanged and existing tests continue to pass.
- Documentation: Start-up instructions and config examples (proxy/config.yaml, start-llama.sh, container notes) are updated to show how to enable co-residency.

- Memory/regression: Document expected combined memory footprint for the co-residency configuration and add an automated memory regression test that asserts combined resident memory does not exceed a documented threshold.

Constraints
-----------
- Scope: Limit the change to supporting `embeddings` + exactly one other model (special-case co-residency) to reduce scope and risk.
- Runtime: Implementation may modify start scripts or container config to enable co-loading; repository start-llama.sh and container images may be updated (acceptable per stakeholder guidance).
- Backwards-compatibility: Do not change behavior for unrelated model names; aliasing must be additive.
- Memory/safety: The implementation must avoid excessive memory use or global state that prevents stable operation. Any hard memory limits or thresholds should be documented and tested.

- Memory threshold: The exact combined memory limit for co-residency is TBD; implementation must document observed footprints and recommend a safe threshold for operators.

Existing state
--------------
- Work item LP-0MN7T0KFJ004ALGK created with the seed description: "Currently the proxy only allows one model to be loaded at a time..." (stage: idea, status: in-progress, assignee: Map).
- The proxy already has a dedicated `/v1/embeddings` handler and model resolution code in `proxy/server.py`. Key helpers exist: `get_model_config()`, `get_local_model_name()`, `ensure_model_loaded()` and `proxy_to_local()`.
- There is an existing completed work item adding the embeddings model alias and example config: "Add an embeddings specific model" (LP-0MN557XBD0H8B8PC). See its intake and tests for context.
- `proxy/MODEL_ADD.md` documents how to add models, alias handling, and an example `mxbai-embed-large-v1` entry; `proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py` contains a basic integration test for the embeddings alias.

Desired change
--------------
- Implement co-residency for `embeddings` + one other model by enabling dynamic, on-demand load of the embeddings model into a running llama-server process (preferred approach chosen in intake).
  - Allow the running llama-server to host the embeddings model alongside a primary model with minimal disruption.
  - Update `ensure_model_loaded()` / `start_llama_server()` / `start-llama.sh` and container notes as required to support co-loading or multi-model invocation.
- Routing & aliasing:
  - Treat omitted `model` on `/v1/embeddings` as `model: "embeddings"` (decision from intake).
  - Ensure `get_model_config()` continues to resolve alias `embeddings` → canonical model for the embed model.
- Tests and validation:
  - Add a concurrent integration test (proxy/tests/test_embeddings_concurrent.py or extend existing) that issues 5 concurrent embedding requests and 5 concurrent non-embedding requests and validates responses.
  - Keep existing embedding integration test and ensure non-regression.
- Documentation: Update `proxy/MODEL_ADD.md`, `proxy/CONTAINERS.md` (if needed), and add a short section to `proxy/README.md` describing how to enable co-residency.

Related work
------------
- proxy/MODEL_ADD.md — guidance for adding models and aliases; includes an example `mxbai-embed-large-v1` (relevant configuration and integration-test guidance). (proxy/MODEL_ADD.md)
- proxy/server.py — contains `/v1/embeddings` endpoint, model resolution (`get_model_config()`), and model lifecycle functions (`ensure_model_loaded()`, `start_llama_server()`). (proxy/server.py)
- proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py — existing integration test verifying `model: "embeddings"` returns OpenAI embeddings format. (proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py)
- Work item: "Add an embeddings specific model" (LP-0MN557XBD0H8B8PC) — completed intake and tests that add the `mxbai-embed-large-v1` alias and integration test; provides context and example config.
- Work item: "Document process for adding a new model to the proxy" (LP-0MN56E9T40NO2QML) — related documentation work.

Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers
--------------------------------------
- Q: "I found these related docs and work items — please confirm if any of them already represent the work to be done, then answer the configuration questions below." — Answer (user): "No — this is distinct (co-residency investigation)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "How should the small embeddings model be hosted so it can co-exist with another model? (pick one)" — Answer (user): "Co-load in same llama-server process (Advanced)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "LP-0MN557XBD0H8B8PC left this open: if `/v1/embeddings` requests omit the `model` field, should the proxy treat that as `model: \"embeddings\"`?" — Answer (user): "Treat omission as `model: \"embeddings\"` (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "When you chose 'Co-load in same llama-server process', which behavior do you prefer?" — Answer (user): "Dynamically load embed model into running llama-server on demand". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "Should co-residency be limited or generalized?" — Answer (user): "Limit to 'embeddings' + exactly one other model (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "Which acceptance tests should be included? (pick any)" — Answer (user): "Add concurrent integration test (2) (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "When both models are co-resident, how should the proxy behave for routing?" — Answer (user): "Allow switching when needed". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "For the concurrent integration test, what concurrency level should we use?" — Answer (user): "5 concurrent requests each (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "Is it acceptable to modify local startup scripts or container config to support co-loading models?" — Answer (user): "Yes — modify start-llama.sh / containers (Recommended)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

Notes and open questions
------------------------
- No OPEN QUESTIONS remain after the intake: the omission-behaviour for `/v1/embeddings` was answered (treat omission as model: "embeddings") and the co-residency approach (dynamic on-demand co-load into running llama-server) and scope (embeddings + exactly one other model) were chosen.

## Acceptance Criteria

## Minimal Implementation

## Deliverables

data.title: Observability & memory measurement

data.description:
Co-residency spike: llama-server API & memory

Headline
--------
Run a focused 2–3 day investigation to validate whether llama-server can co-load the small embeddings model alongside one primary model (embeddings: `mxbai-embed-large-v1`, primary: `gpt-oss-120b`), measure combined memory footprint, and produce a recommendation (co-load supported vs not) with next-step options.

Problem statement
-----------------
The proxy currently starts or switches a single model in the local llama-server, which forces model swaps when embedding requests are routed to the embeddings model. This spike will validate whether llama-server supports co-residency (embeddings + one primary) and measure the memory and operational characteristics needed to make an implementation decision. Note: requirement relaxed — co-residency implemented via router mode (child processes) is acceptable; same-process co-load is not required.

Users
-----
- Frontend engineers and UI tests that call `/v1/embeddings` and need low-latency embedding responses without triggering primary-model swaps.
- Backend services and test harnesses that rely on local embeddings during CI or dev runs.
- Operators running local or containerized environments who need guidance on startup/config and expected memory impact.

Example user stories
- As a frontend developer, I POST `/v1/embeddings` (model omitted or `model: "embeddings"`) and receive a valid embeddings response without the proxy switching away from the primary model.
- As an operator, I can follow the runbook to start llama-server with both the embeddings and primary model available (or understand why this is not feasible).

Success criteria
----------------
- Feasibility: Confirm whether llama-server allows loading `mxbai-embed-large-v1` and `gpt-oss-120b` concurrently (either in the same process or via router-mode child processes) without degrading request availability.
- Measurement: Produce reproducible peak and steady-state memory figures (RSS, and PSS if available) for the co-resident setup and record them in `.opencode/tmp/co-residency-spike-results.md`.
- Recommendation: Clear recorded decision: "co-load supported" or "co-load not supported" and short list of recommended next steps (prototype approach, or alternative pattern like fast on-demand restart or lightweight separate process).
- Observability: Capture any llama-server HTTP endpoints or management calls used (and any missing APIs) and list required API changes or wrapper/workarounds.

Constraints
-----------
- Timebox: 2–3 days for investigation and reporting (no prototype code required in this spike).
- Scope: Limit to one embeddings model + one primary model pair (`mxbai-embed-large-v1` + `gpt-oss-120b`).
- Non-regression: Do not propose changes that would break current proxy routing semantics; changes must be documented and isolated for follow-up implementation work.

Existing state
--------------
- The parent work item (LP-0MN7T0KFJ004ALGK) already captures the larger feature intent: enable co-residency for `embeddings` + one other model, add concurrent integration tests, and update documentation.
- The proxy contains model lifecycle helpers and endpoints in `proxy/server.py` (`get_model_config()`, `ensure_model_loaded()`, `start_llama_server()`) and an existing embeddings alias/test (`proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py`).
- Documentation and examples: `proxy/MODEL_ADD.md`, `proxy/CONTAINERS.md`, and `proxy/README.md` document model config and startup that will be referenced during the spike.

Desired change (spike scope)
---------------------------
- Validate llama-server runtime capabilities: enumerate available HTTP endpoints for model metadata and lifecycle, and test whether models can be available concurrently (same-process or via router-mode child processes).
- Measure memory for the chosen pair and produce reproducible numbers and logs saved to `.opencode/tmp/co-residency-spike-results.md`.
- Produce a short recommendation and clear next-step options: (A) implement router mode or co-load in same process — proceed to prototype and integration tests, (B) co-load not supported — propose alternative (fast on-demand restart or separate process) and tradeoffs.

Related work
------------
- proxy/server.py — core routing and lifecycle helpers; primary implementation surface for any changes. (proxy/server.py)
- proxy/MODEL_ADD.md — model config and alias patterns; example embed model `mxbai-embed-large-v1`. (proxy/MODEL_ADD.md)
- proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py — existing integration test for embeddings alias; candidate to extend with concurrent tests. (proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py)
- Parent work item: "Can we load the embed model and another model at the same time?" (LP-0MN7T0KFJ004ALGK) — contains user stories, acceptance criteria, and desired scope. (LP-0MN7T0KFJ004ALGK)
- Completed work item: "Add an embeddings specific model" (LP-0MN557XBD0H8B8PC) — shows aliasing and test examples for the embeddings model.

Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers
--------------------------------------
- Q: "Which model pair should we measure for the spike?" — Answer (user initial): "mxbau-embed-large-v1 and gpt-oss-120b" (user). Source: interactive reply. Revision: user corrected to `mxbai-embed-large-v1` in a follow-up selection. Final accepted answer (user): `mxbai-embed-large-v1` (embeddings) + `gpt-oss-120b` (primary).
- Q: "How should memory and process footprint be measured and recorded?" — Answer (user): not explicitly selected in initial reply; agent will measure peak and steady-state RSS and PSS (if available) and record results. Source: agent inference & repo practices. Final: agent will use `ps`/`/proc/<pid>/status` and include PSS when available; results saved to `.opencode/tmp/co-residency-spike-results.md` (agent inference, confirmed implicitly by user when selecting the investigation deliverable).
- Q: "What should the spike produce as deliverables?" — Answer (user): "B then A as a follow-up" — (user). Interpreted as: produce an investigation report only for this spike (no prototype code) and follow with a prototype in a subsequent task/phase if the recommendation is to proceed. Source: interactive reply. Final: Investigation report only for this spike; prototype deferred.
- Q: "Confirm embed model name: (A) `mxbai-embed-large-v1` (recommended), (B) `mxbau-embed-large-v1`, (C) other" — Answer (user): "A" — (user). Source: interactive reply. Final: `mxbai-embed-large-v1`.
- Q: "Timebox for this spike: accept the existing 2–3 day timebox (A) or specify a different timebox (B — give days)?" — Answer (user): "A" — (user). Source: interactive reply. Final: 2–3 days.
- Research check: "Does llama-server provide any API or similar?" — Answer (agent research): Yes — the proxy code (`proxy/server.py`) queries llama-server HTTP endpoints for model metadata and status; the proxy expects an HTTP-compatible management surface. Source: `proxy/server.py` (lines referencing querying model metadata and endpoints). Final: yes; agent will exercise those endpoints during the spike and record any missing lifecycle APIs as findings.

Notes / open questions
---------------------
- Acceptance criteria updated per stakeholder request: same-process co-load is no longer required; router-mode child-process co-residency is acceptable.

.opencode/tmp/co-residency-spike-results.md will be created by the spike and should include: commands used, logs, peak & steady-state memory numbers (RSS/PSS), llama-server responses to lifecycle/metadata queries, and a succinct recommendation.

data.title: Router-mode llama-server integration

data.description:
Title: Model lifecycle: embed + primary slot

Problem statement
-----------------
The proxy currently supports only a single loaded model in many runtime modes; we need router-mode support for co-residency so the small embeddings model can be dynamically co-loaded alongside exactly one primary model in a running llama-server process. This will avoid model switches for embedding requests and reduce latency and errors for embedding callers.

Users
-----
- Frontend engineers calling /v1/embeddings who want low-latency embedding responses without causing model switches.
- Backend services that perform indexing/search and rely on local embedding generation.
- Operators and local developers who want a documented, configurable way to run embeddings co-resident with a primary model.

Example user stories
- As a frontend developer, when I POST /v1/embeddings (model omitted or model: "embeddings") I receive a valid OpenAI-style embeddings response without the proxy switching away from the primary model.
- As an operator, I can start the proxy with a configuration that allows the embeddings model and a primary model to be resident concurrently and see documented memory guidance for safe operation on my machine.
- As an engineer, I can run a unit/integration test that validates deterministic load/unload transitions for embed + primary in router-mode.

Success criteria
----------------
- Co-residency: The embeddings model and one configured primary model can be resident concurrently in router-mode such that embedding requests do not force a llama-server model switch (verified by unit tests and integration tests).
- Embeddings API compatibility: POST /v1/embeddings with model: "embeddings" (or omitted model) returns a valid OpenAI-style embeddings response including non-empty embedding vectors of the expected dimension.
- Deterministic lifecycle: Unit tests demonstrate deterministic load/unload state transitions for embed + primary (see proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_unit.py and child task for additional coverage).
- Memory guidance & configurability: Document observed combined memory footprints and provide a configurable baseline for machines; default recommended baseline for 96 GiB machines should be provided and the runtime must expose a config option (config.yaml) to set the safe combined RSS threshold.
- Documentation & tests: All related documentation (proxy/MODEL_ADD.md, proxy/README.md, docs/llama-router.md) is updated and the code changes are covered by unit and integration tests; the full project test suite must pass with the new changes.

Constraints
-----------
- Scope: Limit co-residency to the embeddings model + exactly one other model (special-case) to reduce risk.
- Runtime approach: Router-mode only (non-router modes are out of scope for this item). Implementation must support dynamic on-demand co-load into a running llama-server process.
- Memory safety: Co-residency increases resident memory; the implementation must allow operators to configure a combined memory threshold in proxy/config.yaml and must document recommended defaults for common machine sizes (including 96 GiB target baseline).
- Backwards-compatibility: Do not change behaviour for unrelated model names; embedding alias resolution must remain compatible.

Existing state
--------------
- proxy/server.py contains the embeddings endpoint, model-resolution helpers (get_model_config, get_local_model_name, ensure_model_loaded) and router-mode lifecycle code. Lines referencing embeddings and router preload exist (see proxy/proxy/server.py).
- Tests: proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_unit.py contains initial unit tests for embed lifecycle; proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py verifies embeddings integration; proxy/tests/test_embeddings_concurrent.py exists as a separate concurrent integration test (currently linked as dependent work).
- Config & scripts: proxy/config.yaml includes `embeddings_model` and `llama_embed_load_timeout`; start-llama.sh contains model launch logic with an embeddings flag.
- Related work items: Ancestor LP-0MN8LU516006M2SN (Co-residency runtime support); related spike LP-0MN8SI4S5004AQVX; concurrent integration test LP-0MNA7G226009XQBF (blocked). Outbound dependency LP-0MNA7FYH4001ZUR4 (Router-mode llama-server integration) is completed.

Desired change
--------------
- Modify proxy/server.py lifecycle helpers to support a primary slot + an embed slot (or a small multi-model registry) for router-mode.
- Implement locks or asyncio primitives to guard against global state races during on-demand model load/unload in router-mode.
- Ensure `ensure_model_loaded()` supports loading the embed alias without triggering a full primary model switch when co-residency is enabled.
- Add unit tests demonstrating deterministic transitions (router-only); add or link integration tests as separate dependent items (concurrent integration test remains a dependent work item).
- Add config option in proxy/config.yaml to set a safe combined memory RSS threshold; document recommended defaults for 96 GiB machines.
- Update documentation: proxy/MODEL_ADD.md, proxy/README.md, docs/llama-router.md with configuration and operational guidance.

Related work (automated report)
-------------------------------
- proxy/MODEL_ADD.md — Guidance for adding models and aliases; provides integration-test checklist and examples for embeddings (already references mxbai-embed-large-v1). Relevant for config and testing patterns.
- proxy/proxy/server.py — Core routing and lifecycle helpers; contains logic for embeddings alias, router preload, and background load scheduling (see references to `router_load_model`, `router_wait_for_model`, and embeddings endpoints).
- proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_unit.py — Existing unit tests for scheduling and ensure_model_loaded behavior for embed alias; a useful starting point for expanded deterministic lifecycle tests.
- proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py — Integration test verifying embeddings alias returns OpenAI embeddings format.
- proxy/tests/test_embeddings_concurrent.py — Existing concurrent integration test (5 embeddings + 5 chat concurrent) — left as a separate dependent work item per intake decision.
- docs/llama-router.md — Documentation about router-mode behaviour, co-residency guidance and recommended combined RSS baseline in development (example baseline mentioned: combined RSS <= 32 GiB for small dev machines).
- start-llama.sh & proxy/config.yaml — Startup script and configuration entries that may need updates to support co-loading or exposing an embeddings flag and a configurable memory threshold.
- Related work items:
  - LP-0MN8LU516006M2SN — Co-residency runtime support (parent/ancestor)
  - LP-0MN7T0KFJ004ALGK — Can we load the embed model and another model at the same time? (investigation, in-progress)
  - LP-0MNA7G226009XQBF — Concurrent integration test for embeddings (blocked, will remain a dependent item)
  - LP-0MNA7FYH4001ZUR4 — Router-mode llama-server integration (completed)

Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers
---------------------------------------
- Q: "Scope & runtime: should this be router-mode only with dynamic co-load into same llama-server and limit to embeddings + one other model?" — Answer (user): "yes". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.
- Q: "Integration test scope: include the concurrent integration test (5 concurrent embeddings + 5 concurrent non-embedding) in this work item or leave as a separate dependent task?" — Answer (user): "do not include" (kept separate). Source: interactive reply. Final: keep separate; related dependent item: LP-0MNA7G226009XQBF.
- Q: "Memory threshold: should we adopt a fixed baseline or document observed footprints?" — Answer (user): "My machine has 96Gb for LLMs. Make the baseline realistic for that size machine. It must be configurable via the config." Source: interactive reply. Final: Document observed footprints and provide a recommended default baseline targeted at 96 GiB machines; require a configurable setting in proxy/config.yaml.

Notes on evidence and research
- Evidence for existing tests and server hooks discovered by searching repository: proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_unit.py, proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py, proxy/tests/test_embeddings_concurrent.py, proxy/proxy/server.py, proxy/MODEL_ADD.md, docs/llama-router.md, proxy/config.yaml, start-llama.sh.
- Related work items were discovered via Worklog search and wl show for LP-0MNA7G07U0020NDC (ancestors & children). These are referenced above.
data.title: Model lifecycle: embed + primary slot

data.description:
Headline
--------
Add a reliable pytest integration test that starts the proxy and a router-mode llama-server, issues 5 concurrent embedding requests and 5 concurrent chat requests, asserts all responses are HTTP 200 and valid, and measures llama-server RSS to enforce a 90 GB threshold.

Problem statement
-----------------
Concurrent embedding and non-embedding requests can cause model load/unload races or high memory use in router-mode. We need a reproducible integration test that validates the proxy + router-mode llama-server can serve 5 concurrent embedding requests and 5 concurrent chat requests concurrently, and that the llama-server process RSS stays below the 90 GB threshold for the tested configuration.

Users
-----
- Frontend engineers: want confidence that embedding requests do not cause model switches or failures under concurrent load.
  - As a frontend engineer, I can run a local integration test that verifies 5 concurrent embeddings + 5 chat requests succeed so I can validate performance-related changes.
- Backend/infrastructure engineers: want a regression test that catches memory regressions during co-residency changes.
  - As an infra engineer, I can run the test locally and inspect peak RSS measurements to detect memory leaks or config regressions.
- CI maintainers: want a clear policy for heavy tests (local-only by default) so CI is not overloaded.

Success criteria
----------------
1. Test exercises 5 concurrent embedding requests and 5 concurrent chat (non-embedding) requests and asserts HTTP 200 for all 10 responses.
2. Test verifies the embeddings response conforms to the OpenAI embeddings response format (data array, embedding vector present and non-zero) and chat responses return a valid choices/message structure.
3. Test measures peak RSS for the llama-server process (llama-server process RSS only) and fails if peak RSS exceeds 90 GB.
4. Test is runnable locally by developers using repository tooling (instructions in README or test header). It should not be required to run on every PR; per policy this test is local-only by default.
5. All related documentation is updated to reflect the test and how to run it (README, docs/llama-router.md, proxy/MODEL_ADD.md).
6. Full project test suite must pass with the new changes (when the test is executed in the configured environment).

Constraints
-----------
- Measurement method: use llama-server process RSS (e.g., /proc/<pid>/status or ps outputs) — do not attempt to combine RSS across processes.
- Router-mode llama-server must be started for the test (use start-llama.sh router or the existing test harness).
- Test requires access to a model that supports embeddings (repo/config.yaml default: mxbai-embed) or a compatible test harness; ensure local model presets are available before test run.
- Resource intensity: the test may require large memory (up to the threshold) and should not be run in lightweight CI runners. The test is marked local-only by default.

Existing state
--------------
- Router-mode support is implemented: proxy can start llama-server in router mode and manage models via /models and /models/load (see proxy/proxy/server.py and docs/llama-router.md).
- There are existing integration tests that cover embeddings and chat concurrently:
  - proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py — smoke integration test for embeddings + chat.
  - proxy/tests/test_embeddings_concurrent.py — existing concurrent test that issues 5 embedding + 5 chat requests and asserts HTTP 200 for all responses. (Note: this existing test does not appear to measure peak llama-server RSS or enforce the 90 GB threshold.)
- start-llama.sh supports router mode and presets (mxbai-embed, qwen3) used by tests.

Desired change
--------------
Add or extend a pytest integration test that:
- Starts the proxy and a router-mode llama-server (via start-llama.sh router or existing harness) and confirms the embeddings alias is ready.
- Issues 5 concurrent POST /v1/embeddings requests and 5 concurrent POST /v1/chat/completions requests (use qwen3 for chat requests by default).
- Verifies all responses are HTTP 200 and validate payload formats (OpenAI embeddings format and chat response format).
- Measures and records peak RSS for the llama-server process and asserts the measured peak RSS <= 90 * 2**30 bytes (90 GB).
- Provides clear instructions/comments for running the test locally and guidance for artifact collection on failure (write memory stats and request/response bodies to test logs).

Related work (automated report)
------------------------------
- LP-0MN8LU516006M2SN — Co-residency runtime support (parent feature). Status: open, stage: plan_complete. This feature implements the runtime model lifecycle and is the parent of this work item.
- LP-0MNA7FYH4001ZUR4 — Router-mode llama-server integration. Status: completed. Evidence: proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py and proxy/proxy/server.py implement router-mode startup and basic routing.
- LP-0MNJEG6BU005ZTYY — Concurrent integration test for embeddings. Status: completed. Evidence: proxy/tests/test_embeddings_concurrent.py was added and asserts 5 concurrent embedding + 5 chat requests return HTTP 200. Consider whether this item satisfies the current need or whether LP-0MNA7G226009XQBF must extend it to include peak RSS measurement and the 90 GB assertion.
- proxy/tests/test_embeddings_concurrent.py — Existing test file that implements concurrent request checks (5 embeddings + 5 chat). See lines implementing ThreadPoolExecutor and assertions for HTTP 200.
- proxy/tests/test_embeddings_integration.py — Smoke integration test for embeddings + chat and OpenAI-format checks.
- docs/llama-router.md — Documentation for running llama-server in router mode and preload configs.
- proxy/MODEL_ADD.md — Guidance for adding embeddings models and mentions adding integration tests for the embeddings alias.

Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers
---------------------------------------
- Q: "Please confirm whether any of the above work items (IDs listed) represent the work you intend LP-0MNA7G226009XQBF to cover. If LP-0MNA7G226009XQBF is a duplicate of an existing closed item (e.g., LP-0MNJEG6BU005ZTYY), do you want me to:
  A) Close / mark LP-0MNA7G226009XQBF as duplicate and link to the existing completed item, or
  B) Proceed with intake (LP-0MNA7G226009XQBF) because it has different scope / needs further work (please describe differences)?"
  - Answer: OPEN QUESTION — no response received during this intake. (Asked by agent; awaiting user confirmation). Final: OPEN.

- Q: "Memory threshold target: The work item calls out \"fails if combined resident memory exceeds 90 GB\". Which measurement should the test assert?
   - A) llama-server process RSS only (recommended / simplest)
   - B) Combined RSS of llama-server + proxy processes
   - C) Other — please specify"
  - Answer (user): "A" — llama-server process RSS only. Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

- Q: "Non-embedding request target: Which non-embedding (chat) model should the concurrent non-embedding requests use?
   - A) qwen3 (used in existing tests; recommended)
   - B) The configured primary model from proxy/config.yaml (use current default)
   - C) Other — please specify"
  - Answer (user): "A" — qwen3. Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

- Q: "CI execution policy: Given high memory and resource cost, where should this test run?
   - A) As part of the existing integration test job (PR/CI) — runs on every PR/merge
   - B) Separate CI job (e.g., nightly/infrequent) — recommended for heavy tests
   - C) Local-only (developers run manually)
   - D) Other — please specify"
  - Answer (user): "c" — Local-only (developers run manually). Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

Notes on idempotence
--------------------
- This draft appends the Appendix and Related work report to the work item description and is safe to re-run: the `wl update --description-file` step will overwrite the description rather than append duplicate Appendix entries. If the duplicate question (above) is answered later, the Appendix will be updated rather than duplicated.

data.title: Observability: memory & model metrics

data.description:
Problem statement
---------------
Automated model routing is required so incoming LLM requests are sent to an appropriate model (local OSS or hosted) based on request complexity. Complexity will be derived from the repository's existing effort-and-risk automation and mapped to model targets using a risk-forward weighted score.

Users
-----
- Request authors (engineers, product owners, automation): "As a requester, I want my planning or implementation requests routed to a model appropriate for the task complexity so results are higher quality and cost is predictable." 
- Automation/agents (OpenCode/Worklog integrations): "As an automated agent, I want deterministic routing based on the effort/risk estimate so downstream tooling can rely on predictable model capability/latency." 
- Operators/Finance: "As an operator, I want observable model usage metrics so I can monitor cost and capacity impact." 
- Auditors/Owners: "As an owner, I want traceable routing decisions attached to request records so I can understand why a request used a given model." 

Success criteria
----------------
- Compute a risk-forward weighted score from normalized effort and risk: `score = 0.4*effort_norm + 0.6*risk_norm` and map to models using configurable thresholds. Default mapping: 0–50 -> GPT-mini/OSS (low), 51–80 -> GPT 5.4-codex (medium), 81–100 -> Opus (high).
- Routing uses the existing `effort_and_risk` automation as the primary signal; do not reimplement scoring logic.
- Emit aggregated Prometheus metrics: `routing_model_selected_total{model,env}`, `routing_latency_seconds` (histogram), and `routing_score_histogram` (low-cardinality labels only). Metrics must be scrapeable at the proxy's `/metrics` endpoint (or the project's existing metrics endpoint).
- Unit and integration tests that verify mapping for representative inputs, routing latency measurement, and metrics emission.

Constraints
-----------
- Use the repo's existing effort-and-risk automation (skill and orchestrator) as the authoritative source for normalized values.
- Store routing configuration in the project's local configuration system (do not use Worklog for configuration). Suggested location: the repo's existing config mechanism (e.g., `config/` or other project-standard place) — implementors must follow the project's config conventions.
- Cost controls are monitor-only for initial rollout (record usage, do not block requests).
- Routing explainability will be provided via concise Prometheus metrics and short routing notes in the local request metadata store if required by the project. Do not send per-request full event payloads to external webhooks for this effort.

Request metadata requirement
---------------------------
Because the proxy will not have direct access to the effort_and_risk orchestrator outputs, the requestor must provide the normalized `effort` and `risk` values in the request metadata. Implementors should validate these fields on receipt and use them as the authoritative signal for routing. Suggested minimal metadata schema (JSON-style fields):

- `effort_norm` (number) — normalized effort value, recommended scale 0–100
- `risk_norm` (number) — normalized risk value, recommended scale 0–100
- `score_source` (string, optional) — source identifier (e.g., `effort_and_risk-orch`)
- `estimate_id` (string, optional) — id or reference to the estimate record
- `estimate_timestamp` (string, optional, ISO8601) — when the estimate was produced

Validation rules (suggested): reject non-numeric or out-of-range values; if fields are missing, follow the project's policy for defaults (OPEN QUESTION below).

Default policy for missing/invalid metadata
------------------------------------------
Policy chosen: accept the request and apply safe defaults, and record a warning metric/log for operator review.

- Default values: `effort_norm = 50`, `risk_norm = 50` (midpoint, minimal bias).
- On receipt of missing/invalid metadata the proxy must:
  - Substitute the default values for routing decisions.
  - Emit a low-cardinality warning metric `routing_missing_metadata_total{env}` incremented per missing/invalid event.
  - Emit a log entry at WARN level with the request id (if available) and which fields were missing/invalid.
  - Tag the emitted metrics with `model` only after routing decision is made; do not add per-request identifiers as metric labels.

This policy prioritizes availability while creating operator-visible signals to detect and remediate missing metadata.

Existing state
--------------
- Seed intake present (this draft) that captures mapping strategy and high-level requirements.
- The repository references an `effort_and_risk` skill and orchestrator scripts that capture provisional and formal estimates. Those orchestrator scripts expose a JSON output with normalized `effort_norm` and `risk_norm` values; use their docs and invocation patterns.
- The OpenCode server and TUI (the 'O' pane) are a natural request source; they provide integration points for routing.
- Local model guidance exists in repo documentation (local-first guidance may exist in `LOCAL_LLM.md` in other projects; check this repo for equivalent guidance).

Desired change
--------------
Implement a routing component in the model-proxy/OpenCode integration layer that:
1. Reads per-request context and identifies the originating request/prompt metadata.
2. Calls or consumes the `effort_and_risk` orchestrator (or reads its output) to obtain `effort_norm` and `risk_norm`.
3. Computes the risk-forward weighted `score` and selects a model per configured thresholds.
4. Reads routing configuration from the project's existing config system (implementors should map the project's convention to a file like `config/routing.yaml` or similar).
5. Emits Prometheus metrics (selection counts, routing latency histogram, score histogram) with low-cardinality labels only.
6. Add unit and integration tests verifying score -> model mapping, metrics emission, and reasonable latency measurement.

Related work
------------
- Potentially related docs:
  - `AGENTS.md` — project agent rules and references to `effort_and_risk` automation.
  - `proxy/AGENTS.md` — proxy-specific agent guidance found in this repo.
- Potentially related work items:
  - `Routing & alias behaviour for embeddings` (LP-0MN8LU54M00774GG) — routing behaviour for embeddings.
  - `Router-mode llama-server integration` (LP-0MNA7FYH4001ZUR4) — router API and proxy pre-loading/routing concerns.
  - `Co-residency spike: llama-server API & memory` (LP-0MN8SI4S5004AQVX) — local model co-residency and memory constraints.

Appendix: Clarifying questions asked during intake
-----------------------------------------------
- Q: "Confirm the repo-level routing config file path & format. Pick one (or provide a different path/format):" — Answer (user): "There may be some confusion... Ignore all references to worklog that are about configuration and operation of the model selection. Worklog should only be used to send the LLM request; in this issue we are concerned with how to process that request in this project once received. Therefore all config and similar should be clocal to this project. Use the existing config system." Source: interactive reply. Final: yes. (Note: this overrides earlier suggestions to store config in `.worklog/routing.yaml`.)
- Q: "When should the routing note be appended and which fields must it include? Pick one (or provide a different policy):" — Answer (user): "We should not be appending to the worklog item ONLY to the prometheus monitoring" Source: interactive reply. Final: yes. (Decision: do not append routing notes to Worklog; rely on Prometheus metrics and local metadata if the project requires.)
- Q: "Confirm Prometheus metrics naming, label conventions, and scrape endpoint. Pick one (or provide specifics):" — Answer (user): "Use recommended low-cardinality metrics (Recommended)" Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

- Q: "Proxy access to effort_and_risk outputs — who provides the normalized scores and how are they delivered?" — Answer (user): "The proxy will not have access to those scores. We will need the requestor to provide them in request meta-data; this issue will have to define how that is done." Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

- Q: "If request metadata is missing or invalid, what should the proxy do?" — Answer (user): "Option 2: Accept the request and apply safe defaults (effort_norm=50, risk_norm=50) and record a warning metric/log for operator review." Answering party: user (interactive). Final: yes.

OPEN QUESTIONS (items that still require confirmation before implementation)
OPEN QUESTIONS (items that still require confirmation before implementation)
- Exact config file path & format inside this repo's config system (implementor should follow the project's convention; specify where to add routing config: e.g., `config/routing.yaml` or `etc/routing.yaml`).
 - Resolved: Acceptance criteria below define the initial config location and schema to implement. Implementors must wire the config into the project's canonical config loader.

- If the project requires routing notes in a local request metadata store (instead of Worklog), confirm the exact storage mechanism and fields to include (model, score, reason, timestamp, originating_request_id).
 - Resolved: Initial rollout will not persist per-request routing notes to Worklog or other external ticketing systems. The proxy may keep routing decision details in-memory for request processing and include them in logs if needed. If persistent per-request routing records are later required, a follow-up work item will specify the storage mechanism and schema.

- Confirm Prometheus scrape endpoint if the proxy will not expose `/metrics` directly (use existing service metrics endpoint or a Pushgateway).
 - Resolved: Acceptance criteria below require the proxy to expose a `/metrics` endpoint (or integrate with the project's existing metrics endpoint) and use low-cardinality labels. Using a Pushgateway is allowed only if the project's deployment constraints require it; document pushgateway usage in config.

Accepted implementation decisions & final acceptance criteria
---------------------------------------------------------
1. Config location & format
   - Place the routing configuration in the project's canonical config area. Initial concrete file: `config/routing.yaml` (YAML). The file must declare weights, thresholds, model aliases, Prometheus metric names, and env label defaults. Example keys: `weights: {effort: 0.4, risk: 0.6}`, `thresholds: {low: 50, medium: 80}`, `models: {low: "gpt-mini-oss", medium: "gpt-5.4-codex", high: "opus"}`, `metrics: {endpoint: "/metrics", env: "dev"}`.

2. Routing note persistence
   - Initial rollout: Do NOT append routing notes to Worklog or other ticketing systems. Rationale: user requested Prometheus-first explainability and to avoid per-request external payloads. Routing decisions must be logged at WARN/INFO level (per policy) but not persisted to Worklog. If the project later requires persistent records, create a new work item specifying storage and schema.

3. Prometheus details
   - Proxy must expose a scrapeable `/metrics` endpoint (or register with the project's common metrics endpoint). Metric names and types:
     - `routing_model_selected_total{model,env}` — counter incremented on each routing decision (label cardinality: model, env).
     - `routing_latency_seconds` — histogram measuring routing decision latency (use reasonable buckets: 0.001,0.01,0.1,1,5).
     - `routing_score_histogram{env}` — histogram of computed routing scores (buckets e.g., 0..100 by 10s).
     - `routing_missing_metadata_total{env}` — counter for missing/invalid metadata events.
   - Labels must remain low-cardinality: only `model` and `env` (do not add request ids, user ids, or other high-cardinality labels).
   - If Pushgateway is required, allow configuration in `config/routing.yaml` and document trade-offs.

4. Metadata schema and validation
   - The proxy expects request metadata fields `effort_norm` and `risk_norm` (numbers, 0–100). Optional advisory fields: `score_source`, `estimate_id`, `estimate_timestamp`.
   - Validation: numeric, in-range. Missing or invalid fields cause defaults to be applied (`effort_norm=50`, `risk_norm=50`) and `routing_missing_metadata_total` incremented. Log WARN including request id when available.

5. Tests and observability
   - Unit tests must exercise: valid metadata mapping to each model threshold, missing/invalid metadata fallback behaviour, and metric emission for model selection and missing metadata.
   - Integration tests must validate: end-to-end routing decision for sample inputs, metrics exposed at `/metrics`, and routing latency measurement.

File saved to: `.opencode/tmp/intake-draft-Route-requests-to-models-LP-0MNA8FH17000NN3Z.md`
data.title: Route requests to models based on effort & risk (risk-forward weighted score)
