# Add voice support to Proxy

## Problem statement

The proxy currently only handles text-based LLM endpoints (chat completions, embeddings). Pi, the coding agent, needs to be able to voice (read aloud) its responses to users, but there is no local TTS endpoint available. We need to add TTS generation capability to the proxy so that Pi (and other clients) can request speech synthesis through the same local infrastructure.

## Users

- **Pi coding agent users**: Pi agents will consume TTS audio generated by the proxy to voice content to the user (e.g., reading responses aloud).
- **Proxy operators**: Anyone running the proxy locally will be able to use the TTS endpoint without depending on cloud APIs.

**User story:** "As a Pi user, when Pi has completed a response, I want Pi to speak it aloud so I can listen rather than read. As an operator, I want this to work fully locally using open-weight models without sending data to third-party APIs."

## Acceptance Criteria

1. A `/v1/audio/speech` endpoint is available on the proxy that accepts OpenAI-compatible TTS request format (`POST /v1/audio/speech` with `model`, `input`, `voice`, `response_format` parameters) and returns audio content.
2. The TTS endpoint uses the Qwen3-TTS 1.7B model (Apache 2.0) running locally via the `qwentts.cpp` `tts-server` on the same machine as the proxy.
3. The proxy routes `/v1/audio/speech` requests to the local `tts-server` (running on a configurable port/host) and returns the generated audio to the caller.
4. The `tts-server` is launched and managed alongside the existing `llama-server` lifecycle (startup, health checks, shutdown).
5. Audio output supports WAV format. Additional formats (MP3, Opus) via OpenAI's `response_format` parameter are nice-to-have and deferred.
6. Full project test suite must pass with the new changes.
7. All related documentation is updated to reflect the changes, including proxy README, config.yaml (new TTS server settings), and any relevant wiki or docs site entries.

## Constraints

- **Model must be open weights and commercially friendly**: OuteTTS models (which llama.cpp's built-in `llama-tts` uses) are CC-BY-NC and cannot be used. Qwen3-TTS (Apache 2.0) is the recommended alternative, running via the separate `qwentts.cpp` project.
- **Fully local**: No cloud API dependencies. The TTS server must run on the same machine as the proxy, using only local resources.
- **Same-server deployment**: The TTS server must co-exist with `llama-server` and the proxy without port conflicts or excessive GPU memory contention.
- **Language**: Primary focus is English TTS. Qwen3-TTS supports 10+ languages, but multi-language support is not a hard requirement for v1.

## Risks & assumptions

- **GPU memory contention**: Running a separate TTS server alongside llama-server may increase GPU memory usage. The Qwen3-TTS 1.7B Q8_0 model adds ~2.4GB GPU memory pressure. Mitigation: the 90GB GPU has sufficient headroom, and the server can fall back to CPU-only mode if needed.
- **Scope creep — Pi extension**: The existing `@wenjinnn/pi-mimo-voice` extension provides TTS via remote API. Modifying it to use the proxy's local endpoint is potential follow-up work. Mitigation: record this as a separate work item rather than expanding scope.
- **Scope creep — STT (speech-to-text)**: Speech recognition is explicitly deferred. The existing Pi extensions already provide local STT via whisper.cpp. Mitigation: out of scope for this item; link from a follow-up work item if needed.
- **qwentts.cpp maturity**: The project has 52 GitHub stars and is relatively new. The build may require debugging on this specific GPU/ROCm setup. Mitigation: the codebase is a well-structured C++17/GGML project similar to llama.cpp, and the machine already has GGML infrastructure built.
- **ROCm/HIP compatibility**: This machine uses AMD GPUs with ROCm (gfx1151). qwentts.cpp's CMake does not explicitly handle the `GGML_HIP` backend, so building with GPU acceleration may require CMake adjustments. Mitigation: can fall back to CPU-only mode (GGML CPU backend is always available), or use the CrispASR runtime as an alternative TTS backend if qwentts.cpp cannot be built with ROCm.

## Existing state

- The proxy currently exposes `/v1/chat/completions`, `/v1/completions`, `/v1/embeddings`, `/v1/models` endpoints.
- llama.cpp's `llama-server` does not have a `/v1/audio/speech` endpoint. Its TTS support is limited to the standalone `llama-tts` binary which only works with OuteTTS (CC-BY-NC license).
- The existing `@wenjinnn/pi-mimo-voice` Pi extension provides TTS via the Xiaomi MiMo V2.5 **remote API**, not local.
- `qwentts.cpp` exists as an independent C++17/GGML project with an `tts-server` binary that implements `POST /v1/audio/speech` using Qwen3-TTS models (Apache 2.0). It supports CUDA, ROCm, CPU, Metal, Vulkan.

## Desired change

1. Build `qwentts.cpp` on this machine with CUDA support (matching the existing llama.cpp setup).
2. Download the Qwen3-TTS 1.7B Base model (talker) + Qwen3-TTS Tokenizer GGUF files (Apache 2.0).
3. Create a new `proxy/scripts/start-qwentts.sh` startup script (or extend `start-llama.sh`) to manage the `tts-server` lifecycle.
4. Add a new `/v1/audio/speech` route to the proxy (`proxy/proxy/server.py`/`handlers.py`) that forwards requests to the `tts-server` backend.
5. Add TTS server configuration to `proxy/config.yaml` (host, port, model paths).
6. Update `proxy/README.md` with the new endpoint documentation, and add instructions for building/running the TTS server.
7. Optionally, create a Pi extension that uses the proxy's `/v1/audio/speech` endpoint for local TTS (the existing `@wenjinnn/pi-mimo-voice` could be adapted, or a new minimal extension built).

## Related work

- **`ServeurpersoCom/qwentts.cpp`** (GitHub): Independent C++17/GGML port of Qwen3-TTS. Provides `tts-server` binary with OpenAI-compatible `/v1/audio/speech` endpoint. MIT license for code, uses Apache 2.0 models. Primary candidate for integration.
- **llama.cpp `tools/tts/README.md`** (`/home/rgardler/llama.cpp/tools/tts/README.md`): Documents llama.cpp's built-in TTS support using OuteTTS models. Not suitable due to CC-BY-NC license.
- **`@wenjinnn/pi-mimo-voice`** (npm): Existing Pi extension providing voice input/output using Xiaomi MiMo V2.5 remote API. Could be adapted to route TTS through the local proxy instead. Uses whisper.cpp locally for STT.
- **`@khimaros/pi-omni`** (npm): Another Pi voice extension, provides realtime voice chat.
- **`cstr/qwen3-tts-1.7b-base-GGUF`** (HuggingFace): Alternative GGUF builds of Qwen3-TTS for the CrispASR runtime. Same Apache 2.0 model, different runtime.

## Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers

- **Q1**: "The OuteTTS models supported by llama.cpp are CC-BY-NC (not commercially friendly). What is your preferred approach?" — **Answer (user)**: "c" (Find/research other TTS models that work with llama.cpp's pipeline). **Research**: Qwen3-TTS (Apache 2.0) via `qwentts.cpp` is the recommended alternative. Source: HuggingFace model cards and GitHub repository inspection. Resolved.
- **Q2**: "Scope — just TTS, or also STT?" — **Answer (user)**: "unknown, research extensions that are available and, if necessary build one." **Research**: `@wenjinnn/pi-mimo-voice` Pi extension already handles STT locally via whisper.cpp. `@khimaros/pi-omni` also provides voice features. STT scope is deferred — the existing extensions may suffice without proxy-level STT for v1. Not resolved for this work item; treat STT as out of scope.
- **Q3**: "Pi integration: Should TTS run on the same machine? Local only?" — **Answer (user)**: "same server local only." The TTS server must run locally on the same machine as the proxy/llama-server. Resolved.
- **Q4**: "Model size preference: 0.6B or 1.7B Qwen3-TTS?" — **Answer (user)**: "Go with your recommendation at a higher quality model." — Use Qwen3-TTS 1.7B Base (Q8_0 quantized, ~2.1GB + 291MB tokenizer). Resolved.
data.title: Add voice support to Proxy

data.description:
# Intake Brief: Improve Slot Management (LP-0MQR0780Z006TLX6)

## Headline

Investigate slot-thrashing in the proxy's slot allocation and produce a design proposal for a reservation or queue-based mechanism that prevents inter-session slot stealing and preserves cache across turns.

---

## Problem Statement

When multiple agents make concurrent requests through the proxy, the current slot-allocation system causes thrashing: a slot is released between turns, and another session steals it before the original session's next request arrives. This forces cache rebuilds and model reloads, degrading performance for all active agents. A concrete investigation and design document is needed to define a solution.

---

## Users

- **AI agents** (1–8 concurrent, sharing 1 GPU) making multi-turn requests through the proxy. User story: "As an AI agent, I want my session to hold its slot between turns so my prompt cache is preserved and my responses stay fast."
- **Developers/operators** deploying the proxy. User story: "As an operator, I want to understand how slot contention affects throughput so I can plan capacity and tune configuration."
- **Developer implementing the solution**. User story: "As a developer, I want a clear design proposal with trade-offs analyzed so I can implement the best approach."

---

## Acceptance Criteria

1. **Thrashing verification**: The investigation confirms (or refutes) the thrashing scenario described in the seed context — specifically whether inter-session slot stealing causes cache rebuilds and whether pool_size=1 with the current `SlotLockCoordinator` is sufficient to prevent the problem at the proxy level.

2. **Design proposals**: The deliverable must present **at least two** distinct approaches (e.g., session-level reservation, queue-based allocation, client-side backoff, or a hybrid), each with a clear pros/cons analysis covering:
   - Implementation complexity
   - Impact on cache preservation
   - Fairness across sessions
   - Failure modes and edge cases
   - Compatibility with existing session management (single-flight, delta ingestion)

3. **Recommended approach**: The design document must state a clear recommendation with rationale, including how the approach handles the 1–8 agent caseload with a single GPU and pool_size=1.

4. **Session-end detection analysis**: The design must address how the proxy can know when an agent has finished its turn (timeout, explicit signal, heuristic, etc.) — a key open question from the seed context.

5. **Reproducible evidence**: The investigation must produce reproducible evidence (logs, test harness output, or timing analysis) that any stakeholder can use to verify the findings independently.

6. **Implementation roadmap**: The design document must include a breakdown of the recommended approach into sub-tasks with effort estimates (T-shirt sizing or hours), suitable for creating child work items.

---

## Constraints

- **Single GPU**: Only 1 physical GPU is available, so `slot_pool_size` beyond 1-2 is unlikely to improve throughput (the GPU can only process one inference at a time). The solution must work within this constraint.
- **No changes to llama-server**: The solution must operate at the proxy level; we cannot modify llama-server's slot management.
- **Existing session machinery must remain intact**: Single-flight coordination, delta ingestion, slot save/restore, and session header resolution must continue to function.
- **Backward compatibility**: Existing clients that retry on 503 must not break (though the retry behavior may change with the new approach).

---

## Existing State

### Architecture Overview

The proxy manages slots through several coordinated mechanisms:

1. **Slot availability check** (`_check_slot_availability` in `proxy/proxy/router_helpers.py`, line 488): Before proxying a request, queries llama-server's `/slots` endpoint. If `available_slots == 0` and `total_slots > 0`, returns a 503 JSON response with `Retry-After: 5` header and `no_slots_available` error code.

2. **Slot lock** (`SlotLockCoordinator` in `proxy/proxy/session.py`, line 579): An in-process `asyncio.Lock` per slot_id. With `session_slot_pool_size: 1`, all sessions map to physical slot 0 (via `_slot_id_for_session` SHA-256 hash mod pool_size). This serializes all requests through slot 0.

3. **Slot save/restore** (`_save_slot_snapshot` / `_restore_slot_snapshot` in `proxy/proxy/session.py`): The proxy saves KV cache snapshots to disk after a response and restores them when the next request in the same session arrives. Save is best-effort; restore returns false if the snapshot file doesn't exist.

4. **Session single-flight** (`SessionSingleFlightCoordinator` in `proxy/proxy/session.py`): Only one request per session can be inflight at a time. Concurrent requests for the same session are queued or rejected (mode: queue, max queue depth: 1).

5. **Config** (`proxy/config.yaml`):
   ```yaml
   session_slot_pool_size: 1
   session_slot_timeout_seconds: 3.0
   slot_unavailable_retry_after: 5
   ```

### The Thrashing Scenario

The described thrashing scenario is plausible with the current implementation:

- **Pool_size=1** means all sessions hash to the same physical slot (slot 0).
- **SlotLockCoordinator** serializes concurrent requests through slot 0, but it releases the lock as soon as the streaming/non-streaming response is complete.
- **Between turns**: After Agent A's response is sent and the lock is released, there's a window where Agent B or C can acquire the lock, restore its own cache, and start processing. When Agent A's next request arrives, it finds its cache overwritten and must do a full reprocess or fall back to a full prompt.
- **Concurrent agents (1–8) with 1 GPU**: Since the GPU can only run one inference at a time, `pool_size > 1` wouldn't help — only one agent can be actively processing at any moment. The problem is about *which* agent gets the slot and whether cache is preserved between turns, not about GPU utilization.

### Key Unknowns the Investigation Must Address

1. **Is the thrashing actually observable?** The `SlotLockCoordinator`'s asyncio.Lock should serialize requests through slot 0. But the gap between "response sent" and "next request arrives" is where other sessions can sneak in. The investigation should measure/log this gap.

2. **Does the asyncio.Lock actually prevent the race?** Since `slot_id = hash(session_id) % pool_size`, with pool_size=1 all sessions get slot_id=0, so the same lock serializes everything. When the response stream completes, the lock releases. The moment the lock is released, any waiting asyncio task can acquire it — there's no ordering guarantee.

3. **Are cache rebuilds the dominant cost?** If the slot save/restore is fast (~3s timeout configured), the cost of a stolen slot might be less severe than described. The investigation should measure restore latency.

---

## Desired Change

This work item produces a **design document** and **investigation report**. The expected deliverables are:

1. **Thrashing verification report**: Logs, test runs, or analysis confirming/refuting the thrashing scenario, including timing and conditions.
2. **Design proposal document**: At least 2 distinct approaches with pros/cons analysis and a clear recommendation.
3. **Implementation plan**: A breakdown of the recommended approach into implementable sub-tasks with effort estimates.
4. **Any code/test scaffolding** needed to support the investigation (e.g., test harnesses, logging instrumentation, or reproduction scripts).

The investigation is expected to examine these approaches at minimum:

### Approach A: Session-level slot reservation
The proxy holds a reservation for a session's slot after responding, with a timeout. The slot is only released when:
- The session explicitly signals completion (requires a client-side API change)
- An idle timeout expires
- Another session's request arrives and the slot is needed (preemption)

**Pros**: Best cache preservation for active sessions; intuitive semantics.
**Cons**: Requires session-end detection (heuristic or API); idle timeout tuning is tricky (too short = thrashing, too long = wasted capacity with 1–8 agents sharing 1 GPU).

### Approach B: Queue-based slot allocation
Requests wait in a FIFO or fair queue for the slot, rather than all retrying independently. The queue is managed by the proxy, so when a slot becomes free, the next waiting request gets it in order.

**Pros**: Fairness; no starvation; no session-end detection needed; works with existing Retry-After/503 mechanism.
**Cons**: Queue depth management; doesn't prevent inter-session cache invalidation between turns (still subject to the gap); queue may grow deep with 8 concurrent agents.

### Approach C: Client-side backoff with jitter & coordinated retry
Agents use randomized exponential backoff (with jitter) when retrying after a 503, reducing the probability of synchronized retries. Combined with a small retry-after window that varies per agent.

**Pros**: Zero proxy changes; simple to implement on the client side.
**Cons**: Purely statistical — doesn't guarantee starvation-free behavior; doesn't address cache preservation; shifts complexity to each agent.

### Approach D: Hybrid (reservation + limited queue)
Reserve the slot for the current session for a short window (e.g., 2–5 seconds) after each response. If the session's next request arrives within the window, it reclaims the slot with cache intact. If the window expires, the slot goes to the next waiting request (FIFO queue). The window should be tuned based on typical agent "thinking time."

**Pros**: Combines best of A and B; preserves cache for fast-follow-ups; falls back to fair allocation on idle.
**Cons**: More complex to implement; window duration is workload-dependent and may need auto-tuning.

---

## Related Work

| Item | Title | Relationship |
|------|-------|-------------|
| LP-0MPQ2PLTC000GYRZ | Return informative 503 with Retry-After when no slots available | Completed — defines current slot-exhaustion response; the 503/Retry-After mechanism is what agents retry on |
| LP-0MPYMVEYO0015J5L | Observability: slot-reject signal verification | Completed — verified observability signals for slot rejection path; relevant for measurement |
| LP-0MPU11WBN004MFQ8 | Session Routing Regression Suite | Completed — regression tests covering slot coordination, session routing |
| LP-0MPQ1VMX0000GNF4 | Refactor proxy server | Completed — extracted session.py, router_helpers.py, etc.; this work builds on that structure |
| LP-0MQMC4MKY006J08E | Prompt-cache / session reuse tests & small fixes | Open — related work on session prompt cache preservation |
| LP-0MQMC4MNU002QJK4 | Cleanup: slot-cache retention & cleanup script | Open — related to slot file lifecycle |
| LP-0MQ0PYH8P008DLPJ | Web based logging per slot | Open — related to per-slot visibility |
| LP-0MPN8BALG000DBB3 | Mitigate llama-server ROCm crash path and add backend recovery safeguards | Completed — concurrency gates, slot checks context |

### Key Files

- `proxy/proxy/router_helpers.py` — `_check_slot_availability` (line 488), `_build_slot_exhaustion_response` (line 198)
- `proxy/proxy/session.py` — `SlotLockCoordinator` (line 579), slot save/restore helpers
- `proxy/proxy/router.py` — request routing with slot check (line 202), slot lock acquire (line 244, 811)
- `proxy/proxy/handlers.py` — `poll_slots_for_model` (line 79)
- `proxy/config.yaml` — slot configuration (lines 206-209)
- `proxy/tests/test_slot_polling.py` — existing slot polling tests
- `proxy/tests/test_incremental_ingestion.py` — session ingestion tests with slot context

---

## Risks & Assumptions

- **Risk: Scope creep into full implementation.** The investigation + design could naturally expand into prototyping or production code. Mitigation: Record any discovered implementation needs as separate child work items linked to this item rather than expanding scope.
- **Risk: Complex design that is difficult to implement.** The design space (reservation, queuing, backoff, timeout tuning) could produce a proposal that's too complex to implement reliably. Mitigation: The design document must include a complexity/effort estimate for each approach.
- **Risk: Thrashing may not be as severe as described.** The existing `SlotLockCoordinator` (asyncio.Lock) serializes requests through slot 0 at the proxy level. If the asyncio event loop processes Agent B's retry before Agent A's client sends the next request, the lock serialization might already prevent the most pathological cases. Mitigation: The investigation must measure actual behavior before designing a solution.

- **Risk: Investigation may be inconclusive.** If the thrashing scenario cannot be reliably reproduced (e.g., the asyncio event loop serializes requests tightly enough that the race window is negligible), the design may need to proceed on theoretical or worst-case analysis. Mitigation: Define minimum reproduction criteria upfront; if reproduction fails, base design on worst-case analysis.

- **Risk: Conflating proxy slot management with llama-server's internal slots.** The proxy manages `session_slot_pool_size` (how many slot save/restore contexts) while llama-server manages its own N physical slots for concurrent inference. These are related but separate — the investigation must clarify the boundary between them.

- **Risk: The `pool_size=1` constraint may limit solution space.** Even with a single GPU, `pool_size > 1` might still improve cache management by letting llama-server maintain separate KV cache slots across sessions, even if inference itself is serialized. The investigation should evaluate whether increasing pool_size is viable.

- **Risk: Interference with concurrent slot-related work items.** Open items (LP-0MQMC4MKY006J08E, LP-0MQMC4MNU002QJK4, LP-0MQ0PYH8P008DLPJ) touch the same slot code paths. The investigation may need to coordinate with or incorporate findings from these items.

- **Assumption:** The `SlotLockCoordinator`'s asyncio.Lock serializes requests within the proxy process but cannot prevent the gap between response-sent and next-request-arrived.
- **Assumption:** `session_slot_pool_size` may need to be increased (to be verified by investigation).
- **Assumption:** Existing client agents retry on 503 with some form of backoff (the investigation should verify this).
- **Scope-control risk:** The investigation could uncover additional slot-management issues (cache corruption, save/restore reliability, timeout misconfiguration). These should be recorded as separate work items, not added to the current scope.

---

## Appendix: Clarifying Questions & Answers

- **Q1:** "What is the primary scope of this work?"
  - **A) Investigation + design only** — Verify the thrashing behavior and produce a design proposal for a better slot management approach. Implementation to follow in a separate work item.
  - **B) Investigation + design + implementation** — Same as A, but also implement the chosen approach within this same work item.
  - **C) Implementation only** — The system understanding is already sufficient; focus on implementing a specific solution.
  - **Answer (user):** "A) Investigation + design only". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

- **Q2:** "Do you have a preferred approach direction or constraints?"
  - **Answer (user):** "I am looking for recommendations, it's OK to give options as long as you present pros and cons of each." Source: interactive reply. Final: yes — the design document must present multiple approaches with pros/cons and a recommendation.

- **Q3:** "How many concurrent agents/sessions do we need to support?"
  - **Answer (user):** "1–8, but remember there is only 1 GPU." Source: interactive reply. Final: yes — the design must account for 1 GPU constraint with up to 8 concurrent sessions.
data.title: Improve slot management

data.description:
# Intake Brief: Remove all traces of distrobox

## Problem statement

The proxy codebase and documentation still contain many references to `distrobox` — the legacy container runtime that has been superseded by the podman-based startup mechanism (`scripts/podman_start_llama.sh`). These stale references are actively causing runtime failures: when the host-start path fails, the distrobox fallback in `start_llama_server()` triggers and produces errors like `"distrobox attempt 1 failed quickly: Error: unable to find user rgardler: no matching entries in passwd file"`.

## Users

- **Operators** managing the proxy service — currently receive confusing distrobox-related error messages and diagnostic output from a mechanism that is no longer used.
- **Developers** maintaining the codebase — need to understand that container-based llama-server deployment uses podman directly, not distrobox.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. All runtime code paths that invoke `distrobox` or reference `distrobox_name` are removed from `proxy/proxy/lifecycle.py` (`start_llama_server`, `stop_llama_server`, and any fallback logic in `ensure_model_loaded`).
2. The `distrobox_name` config key is removed from `proxy/config.yaml` and any code that reads it.
3. The `prefer_distrobox` parameter is removed from `start_llama_server()` and all callers in lifecycle.py and server.py; the function starts llama-server exclusively via the configured `llama_start_script` (currently `scripts/podman_start_llama.sh`) and fails hard with a clear error message if that fails.
4. All distrobox-related tests in `proxy/tests/test_lifecycle_host_start.py` and `proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_router_unit.py` are removed or rewritten to exercise the podman-based path.
5. All documentation references to distrobox are removed or updated:
   - `proxy/README.md` — remove distrobox fallback description
   - `LLAMA_README.md` — remove distrobox setup instructions
   - `proxy/MODEL_ADD.md` — replace `distrobox enter` example with podman equivalent
   - `proxy/CONTAINERS.md` — remove "avoiding reliance on distrobox" text
   - `proxy/HOST_INSTALL.md` — remove distrobox mention
   - `docs/systemd/llama-server.service` — remove distrobox comment
   - `proxy/templates/index.html` — update UI text
6. Support scripts are updated:
   - `proxy/scripts/fault-injection-backend-crash.sh` — remove distrobox-specific kill path
   - `proxy/scripts/diagnose-llama-proxy-startup.sh` — remove distrobox from grep patterns and search text
7. Inline comments referencing distrobox are cleaned up in `start-llama.sh`, `proxy/proxy/server.py`, and any other source files.
8. The `distrobox_name` config key is also removed from `proxy/README.md`'s example config block and any other documentation where it appears.
9. All related documentation is updated to reflect the changes, including code comments, README, and any relevant wiki or docs site entries.
10. Full project test suite must pass with the new changes.

## Constraints

- The host-start code in `start_llama_server()` and `ensure_model_loaded()` (lifecycle.py) is currently **blocked** by ROCm/HIP failures tracked in LP-0MQOAQEFP004WD0V. That block must be resolved or a workaround in place before this work item can be fully tested end-to-end. However, the distrobox removal itself can be implemented and unit-tested without the ROCm fix.
- After removal, when `llama_start_script` (currently `scripts/podman_start_llama.sh`) fails to start, the proxy must produce a clear, actionable error message — not a distrobox-related error.
- The `start-llama.sh` fallback on PATH may still be used directly by operators for manual debugging; that is unchanged.

## Existing state

- `proxy/config.yaml` sets `llama_start_script: /home/rgardler/projects/llm/scripts/podman_start_llama.sh` — a podman-based wrapper that creates/runs llama-server inside a container via `podman create/start/exec`.
- `proxy/proxy/lifecycle.py` still has the old distrobox code paths in:
  - `start_llama_server()` (lines ~653–828): builds `distrobox_cmd`, checks `shutil.which("distrobox")`, has a 4-attempt retry loop with `distrobox enter`, and includes distrobox-specific diagnostic messages.
  - `stop_llama_server()` (lines ~875–920): first tries `distrobox enter ... pkill -f llama-server`.
  - `ensure_model_loaded()` (lines ~940–1140): references `prefer_distrobox=False` and has distrobox fallback attempts.
- `proxy/config.yaml` line 172: `distrobox_name: llama`.
- Tests in `test_lifecycle_host_start.py` and `test_model_lifecycle_router_unit.py` exercise distrobox paths (mocked).
- Documentation across 7+ files references distrobox setup, usage, or concepts.

## Desired change

Remove all runtime code, configuration, tests, documentation, and comments referencing distrobox. The proxy's container-based startup is exclusively via `scripts/podman_start_llama.sh` (or whatever `llama_start_script` is configured to). The `start_llama_server()` function should have one path: invoke the configured `llama_start_script` directly; if it fails, report a clear error. No distrobox retry loop, no distrobox_name config, no `prefer_distrobox` parameter.

## Related work

### Related work items

- **LP-0MQNZXZUH002FTON** (Implement host-first llama-server startup with llama_allow_host_fallback) — Blocked by ROCm/HIP failures; this sibling item added the host-first code path but preserved the distrobox fallback. Distrobox removal builds on that work.
- **LP-0MQOAQEFP004WD0V** (Investigate and fix ROCm/HIP failures causing host-start llama-server crashes) — Critical blocking dependency. Host-start must work before distrobox removal can be end-to-end validated.
- **LP-0MQMC3RUC008K6TB** (Robust host-start and distrobox logic) — Sibling task to consolidate spawn helpers; overlaps with this removal (the other direction — consolidating rather than removing).
- **LP-0MQMC3S0D000GP2E** (Document host-first operation and systemd units) — In-progress docs work that covers the transition; may need updates after distrobox removal.
- **LP-0MQMC28PD007HZYS** (Cleanup and hardening after ROCm/host-start work) — Parent epic. This removal is one of the final cleanup steps.

### Related docs & files

- `proxy/proxy/lifecycle.py` — Primary file for distrobox removal (start/stop/fallback logic)
- `proxy/proxy/server.py` — Re-exports, lifespan comment mentioning distrobox/podman boot race
- `proxy/config.yaml` — `distrobox_name: llama` config key (line 172)
- `scripts/podman_start_llama.sh` — The replacement podman-based startup script (already exists)
- `proxy/tests/test_lifecycle_host_start.py` — Tests for distrobox fallback paths
- `proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_router_unit.py` — Test for distrobox-missing scenario
- `proxy/templates/index.html` — UI text mentioning distrobox
- `proxy/README.md` — Documents distrobox fallback
- `proxy/HOST_INSTALL.md` — Mentions distrobox
- `proxy/CONTAINERS.md` — Mentions "avoiding reliance on distrobox"
- `proxy/MODEL_ADD.md` — `distrobox enter` command example
- `proxy/scripts/diagnose-llama-proxy-startup.sh` — Grep patterns for distrobox
- `proxy/scripts/fault-injection-backend-crash.sh` — Uses `distrobox enter ... pkill`
- `LLAMA_README.md` — Full distrobox setup instructions
- `start-llama.sh` — Comment referencing distrobox (line 70)
- `docs/systemd/llama-server.service` — Comment referencing distrobox (line 14)

## Risks & assumptions

- **Scope creep risk**: Removal could expand into refactoring the full startup/stop logic or restructuring the lifecycle module. Mitigation: scope is strictly removing distrobox references and distrobox-only code paths. Any opportunities discovered for additional refactoring, restructuring, or feature work must be recorded as new work items linked to this item or the parent epic, rather than expanding the scope of this item.
- **ROCm/HIP blocking dependency**: The host-start code path (podman_start_llama.sh) is blocked by LP-0MQOAQEFP004WD0V. Without the ROCm fix, end-to-end validation of the distrobox-free path cannot run. Mitigation: unit tests can validate the code paths with mocks; integration testing must wait for ROCm fix.
- **Regression risk**: Removing the distrobox fallback could leave the proxy without any container-based startup if podman is also unavailable. Mitigation: the code should produce a clear, actionable error message explaining that neither host llama-server nor podman container runtime is available, with installation instructions.
- **Test coverage gap**: Existing distrobox tests provide coverage for startup failure scenarios. Simply deleting them without replacement could leave gaps. Mitigation: rewrite tests to cover podman-not-found / podman-start-failure scenarios instead.
- **Assumption**: The `podman_start_llama.sh` script is the sole intended container-startup mechanism going forward. If a different `llama_start_script` is configured, the removal still applies — distrobox is never an acceptable path.
- **Assumption**: No external tooling or operator workflows depend on the distrobox container still being functional. The `distrobox enter llama` workflow is fully replaced by `podman exec` equivalents.
- **Risk**: Removing the `distrobox_name` config key is a breaking change for anyone who still has the old distrobox container. Mitigation: document the removal in the changelog and provide a migration note.
- **Risk**: The distrobox fallback tests currently provide code coverage for the case when no container runtime is available. After removal, these test cases must be adapted to test the podman-not-found path instead.
- **Assumption**: The `prefer_distrobox=False` parameter and its use in callers (`server.py`, `lifecycle.py` tests) can be safely removed because no code path ever passes `True` now that distrobox is being eliminated.

## Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers

- **Q:** "The description mentions 'start-proxy script' but I couldn't find distrobox references in start-proxy.sh. Do you mean the broader proxy codebase?" — **Answer (user)**: Yes, the broader proxy codebase.

- **Q:** "Is the current fallback mechanism podman-based or distrobox-based?" — **Answer (researcher/agent)**: Podman-based. The config.yaml sets `llama_start_script: /home/rgardler/projects/llm/scripts/podman_start_llama.sh`, which uses `podman create/start/exec` directly. The distrobox code paths in lifecycle.py are entirely redundant — they wrap `podman_start_llama.sh` inside a `distrobox enter` call, creating an unnecessary container-within-container nesting. Source: `proxy/config.yaml` line 239, `scripts/podman_start_llama.sh`, `proxy/proxy/lifecycle.py` lines 666–828.

- **Q:** "What scope of distrobox traces should be removed?" — **Answer (user)**: Everything — all runtime code, config, tests, documentation, scripts, UI template, and inline comments. (But contingent on the podman fallback being correct, which was verified.)
data.title: remove all traces of distrobox

data.description:
# Deploy Prometheus and Grafana instances for monitoring infrastructure

**Headline:** Deploy Prometheus (for metrics scraping/storage) and Grafana (for visualization) as systemd user services on the host, configure Prometheus to scrape the proxy `/metrics` endpoint, import existing Grafana dashboard JSON definitions, and document deployment and access instructions.

---

## Problem Statement

The project has created Prometheus metrics endpoints (proxy `/metrics`), alert rule YAML files (in `monitoring/`), and Grafana dashboard JSON definitions (in `monitoring/grafana_llama_memory_dashboard.json`), but there are no running Prometheus or Grafana instances on the host. Without deployed monitoring infrastructure, metrics cannot be scraped, stored, queried, or visualized — making the existing instrumentation effectively invisible to operators.

---

## Users

**Primary:** System operators and developers who maintain the LLM proxy stack and need to observe proxy health, model memory usage, 5xx error rates, and (in future) GPU VRAM metrics.

### User Stories

- As an operator, I want Prometheus scraping the proxy `/metrics` endpoint so that I can query and alert on proxy health, memory usage, and error rates.
- As an operator, I want Grafana running with the existing dashboard imported so that I can visualize proxy and model metrics in a browser.
- As a developer debugging an incident, I want to query historical metrics via Prometheus so that I can correlate events with metric changes.
- As an on-call engineer, I want Prometheus to evaluate existing alert rules so that I can detect memory pressure and 5xx spikes (alert notification routing via Alertmanager is a separate future enhancement).

---

## Acceptance Criteria (Success Criteria)

1. **Prometheus running**: Prometheus is deployed as a systemd user service (following the project's host-first pattern) and is actively scraping the proxy `/metrics` endpoint (`localhost:8000`).
2. **Grafana running**: Grafana is deployed as a systemd user service and is accessible via a web browser on the configured port.
3. **Dashboards imported**: The existing dashboard JSON from `monitoring/grafana_llama_memory_dashboard.json` is imported into Grafana and renders data from Prometheus.
4. **Prometheus datasource configured in Grafana**: Grafana has Prometheus configured as a datasource, enabling dashboard queries and ad-hoc metric exploration.
5. **Metrics queryable**: Prometheus metrics are queryable via `promtool` query, the Prometheus HTTP API, or the Prometheus expression browser UI.
6. **Alert rules loaded**: Prometheus is configured to load and evaluate the existing alert rule files (`monitoring/llama_memory_alerts.yaml` and `monitoring/proxy_5xx_alerts.yaml`). Alerts are visible in the Prometheus UI (Alertmanager deployment is out of scope).
7. **All related documentation is updated** to reflect the changes, including code comments, README, and any relevant wiki or docs site entries.
8. **Full project test suite must pass** with the new changes.

---

## Constraints

- **Host-first deployment**: Deployment follows the project's existing host-first model using systemd user services (aligned with `docs/systemd/llama-server.service` and `docs/systemd/llama-proxy.service`).
- **Binary installation**: Prometheus and Grafana are installed as binary releases (not containers), consistent with the project's host-first pattern. Download official releases from `prometheus.io/download` and `grafana.com/grafana/download`.
- **Single host**: Both Prometheus and Grafana run on the same host as the proxy (no distributed deployment).
- **Default Grafana credentials**: Initial Grafana access uses the default `admin`/`admin` credentials. Operators are instructed to change the password on first login (documented in setup instructions).
- **Prometheus data retention**: Use Prometheus default retention (15 days) unless a specific requirement arises. Configurable via command-line flags.
- **Alertmanager not in scope**: Alertmanager is not deployed as part of this work item. Prometheus records and evaluates alert rules, and alerts are visible in the Prometheus UI/API. Notification routing (email, webhook, etc.) will be addressed in a separate work item if needed.
- **Prometheus as sole Grafana datasource**: Grafana is configured with Prometheus as the only data source. Log aggregation (e.g., Loki) is out of scope for this work item.

---

## Existing State

- The proxy exposes `/metrics` (Prometheus exposition format) with: `llama_process_rss_bytes`, `llama_model_rss_bytes`, `llama_models_loaded`, `proxy_http_errors_total`, and token-rate metrics (`llama_token_rate_gauge`, `llama_token_rate_histogram`).
- `monitoring/grafana_llama_memory_dashboard.json` — A Grafana dashboard JSON with panels for process RSS, models loaded, and proxy 5xx error rate (datasource: Prometheus).
- `monitoring/llama_memory_alerts.yaml` — Prometheus alert rules for process RSS (warning at 75% of 90GB, critical at 90GB).
- `monitoring/proxy_5xx_alerts.yaml` — Prometheus alert rules for 5xx errors (critical when rate > 5 per second over 5 minutes).
- No Prometheus or Grafana instances are running on the host.
- Systemd user service patterns are established via `docs/systemd/llama-server.service` and `docs/systemd/llama-proxy.service`.
- The proxy README (`proxy/README.md`) includes an example Prometheus scrape config and a monitoring section (lines ~1128–1170).

---

## Desired Change

1. **Install Prometheus binary**: Download the official Prometheus release, extract it, and create a systemd user service unit (stored at `~/.config/systemd/user/prometheus.service`). Create a data directory (`~/.local/share/prometheus/`) and configure scrape jobs targeting `localhost:8000` (proxy `/metrics`).
2. **Load existing alert rules**: Configure Prometheus to load `monitoring/llama_memory_alerts.yaml` and `monitoring/proxy_5xx_alerts.yaml` as alerting rule files.
3. **Install Grafana binary**: Download the official Grafana OSS release, extract it, and create a systemd user service unit (stored at `~/.config/systemd/user/grafana-server.service`). Grafana data is stored under its default location (`~/.local/share/grafana/` or the Grafana data directory).
4. **Configure Grafana datasource**: Add Prometheus as a datasource in Grafana via the provisioning API or a pre-configured `datasources.yaml` provisioning file at `${grafana_home}/conf/provisioning/datasources/`.
5. **Import existing dashboard**: Install the dashboard from `monitoring/grafana_llama_memory_dashboard.json` into Grafana via the provisioning API or a pre-configured `dashboards.yaml` provisioning file at `${grafana_home}/conf/provisioning/dashboards/`.
6. **Document**: Create a monitoring deployment guide (at `monitoring/README.md` or updated in `proxy/README.md`) covering:
   - Download, installation, and systemd unit placement
   - Prometheus configuration (scrape config, alert rule files)
   - Grafana configuration (datasource, dashboard provisioning)
   - Service management (`systemctl --user start|stop|status|enable`)
   - Verification steps (curl metrics endpoint, Prometheus targets page, Grafana login and dashboard view)
   - Default credentials notice and password change instructions

---

## Related Work

### Work Items

| ID | Title | Relationship |
|----|-------|-------------|
| LP-0MQMH11AO002IIID | Add GPU VRAM/ROCm metrics to Prometheus and Grafana dashboards | **Dependent** — This deployment is a prerequisite for GPU VRAM metrics to be observable; the VRAM work item assumes Prometheus/Grafana are already deployed |
| LP-0MNA7G5JB004P5O6 | Observability: memory & model metrics | **Foundation** — Created the `metrics.py` module, `monitoring/llama_memory_alerts.yaml`, and `monitoring/grafana_llama_memory_dashboard.json` that this deployment brings to life |
| LP-0MQ1HDY1N00502S7 | Add monitoring and alert for proxy 5xx spikes | **Foundation** — Created `monitoring/proxy_5xx_alerts.yaml` that this deployment loads into Prometheus |
| LP-0MQ53C4HU003FCZ3 | Grafana panel for 5xx counter | **Pattern** — Extended the existing Grafana dashboard JSON demonstrating the dashboard editing pattern |
| LP-0MQ53BRAU000FY39 | Prometheus alert rule for 5xx spikes | **Foundation** — Created `monitoring/proxy_5xx_alerts.yaml` with the alert rule format |
| LP-0MQ53CAME002A6ES | Documentation updates for 5xx monitoring | **Pattern** — Updated `proxy/README.md` monitoring section; this work item will follow the same documentation patterns |
| LP-0MQ53C4HS0058YQK | 5xx runbook for on-call | **Reference** — Runbook for proxy 5xx errors; the deployed Prometheus will make the metrics referenced in the runbook queryable |

### Repository Files

| File | Relevance |
|------|-----------|
| `monitoring/grafana_llama_memory_dashboard.json` | **Target dashboard** — Will be imported into Grafana |
| `monitoring/llama_memory_alerts.yaml` | **Target alert rule** — Will be loaded into Prometheus |
| `monitoring/proxy_5xx_alerts.yaml` | **Target alert rule** — Will be loaded into Prometheus |
| `proxy/README.md` (monitoring section, ~lines 1128–1170) | **Target documentation** — Where the example scrape config and Grafana reference live; will be updated with deployment docs |
| `docs/systemd/llama-server.service` | **Pattern** — Systemd user service pattern to follow for Prometheus and Grafana units |
| `docs/systemd/llama-proxy.service` | **Pattern** — Systemd user service pattern to follow for Prometheus and Grafana units |
| `proxy/proxy/metrics.py` | **Reference** — The metrics module that powers the `/metrics` endpoint Prometheus will scrape |

---

## Appendix: Clarifying Questions & Answers

- **Q1 (Alertmanager deployment):** "Should we deploy Prometheus Alertmanager alongside Prometheus for handling the existing `monitoring/llama_memory_alerts.yaml` and `monitoring/proxy_5xx_alerts.yaml` alert rules, or should we skip Alertmanager for now and just have Prometheus record alerts without notification routing?"  
  **Answer (user):** "B" — Skip Alertmanager for now; Prometheus still records and exposes alerts via its UI/API.  
  Source: Interactive reply. Final: yes.

- **Q2 (Grafana authentication):** "What approach for Grafana authentication?"  
  **Answer (user):** "A" — Default `admin`/`admin` with a documented recommendation to change the password on first login.  
  Source: Interactive reply. Final: yes.

- **Q3 (Additional Grafana data sources):** "Beyond Prometheus, should Grafana be pre-configured with any other data sources (e.g., Loki for log aggregation)?"  
  **Answer (user):** "B if appropriate in this use case - your call."  
  **Agent decision:** Prometheus only. Adding Loki would require deploying a separate log aggregation stack (Loki + promtail/grafana-agent + storage), which is significant new scope beyond this metrics-focused deployment. The project already has rotating file logs and a live log-tail UI (`/logs`, `/logs/tail` SSE) for log access. Log aggregation via Loki is a worthy follow-up but out of scope for this work item.  
  Source: Interactive reply + agent judgment. Final: Prometheus only.

---

## Risks & Assumptions

### Risks

| Risk | Mitigation |
|------|------------|
| **Binary download availability**: Official Prometheus/Grafana download URLs or GPG signatures may change between implementation and deployment | Document specific version numbers and SHA256 checksums in the deployment guide; verify downloads during implementation |
| **Port conflicts**: Prometheus (default 9090) or Grafana (default 3000) ports may be in use on the host | Document port configuration options and verification steps; choose non-conflicting ports if needed |
| **Systemd user service limitations**: systemd user services may not survive system reboots if `linger` is not enabled for the user | Document `loginctl enable-linger` requirement for reboot persistence |
| **Grafana dashboard schema migration**: The imported dashboard JSON uses `schemaVersion: 16` which may not be compatible with newer Grafana versions | Verify dashboard import compatibility with the installed Grafana version during implementation; document any migration steps |
| **Scope creep**: Additional features (Alertmanager, Loki, additional dashboards, multi-host scraping) may be proposed during implementation | Record any additional features as separate work items linked to this item rather than expanding scope |

### Assumptions

- The proxy is already running locally at `localhost:8000` and the `/metrics` endpoint is accessible.
- The operator has `systemctl --user` available and systemd user services are the preferred deployment mechanism.
- Prometheus `--storage.tsdb.retention.time=15d` (default) is acceptable for initial deployment.
- Official binary releases of Prometheus and Grafana OSS are available for the host architecture (amd64).
- The existing `monitoring/grafana_llama_memory_dashboard.json` can be imported via Grafana's provisioning system without schema changes.
- Prometheus alert rule files (`monitoring/*.yaml`) can be loaded via `--rules.file` or `rule_files` in `prometheus.yml`.

---

## Related work (automated report)

### Work items

| ID | Title | Status | Relevance |
|----|-------|--------|-----------|
| LP-0MQMH11AO002IIID | Add GPU VRAM/ROCm metrics to Prometheus and Grafana dashboards | open | **Dependent** — This deployment is explicitly a prerequisite for GPU VRAM metrics to be observable. The VRAM work item's acceptance criteria assume Prometheus and Grafana are already running to scrape and visualize the rocm-exporter metrics. |
| LP-0MNA7G5JB004P5O6 | Observability: memory & model metrics | completed | **Foundation** — Created the `metrics.py` module that powers the proxy `/metrics` endpoint, authored `monitoring/llama_memory_alerts.yaml` and `monitoring/grafana_llama_memory_dashboard.json` — all of which this deployment will bring online. |
| LP-0MQ1HDY1N00502S7 | Add monitoring and alert for proxy 5xx spikes | completed | **Foundation** — Created `monitoring/proxy_5xx_alerts.yaml` that this deployment will load into Prometheus for evaluation. Established the alert rule format used throughout the project. |
| LP-0MQ53C4HU003FCZ3 | Grafana panel for 5xx counter | completed | **Pattern** — Extended the shared Grafana dashboard JSON with a new panel; demonstrates the dashboard editing and provisioning pattern that this deployment's dashboard import will leverage. |
| LP-0MQ53BRAU000FY39 | Prometheus alert rule for 5xx spikes | completed | **Foundation** — Child of LP-0MQ1HDY1N00502S7; created the actual `monitoring/proxy_5xx_alerts.yaml` file that will be loaded into Prometheus. |
| LP-0MQ53CAME002A6ES | Documentation updates for 5xx monitoring | completed | **Pattern** — Updated `proxy/README.md` monitoring section as part of the 5xx monitoring lifecycle; this deployment will similarly update documentation for the deployed services. |
| LP-0MQ53C4HS0058YQK | 5xx runbook for on-call | completed | **Reference** — The runbook references Prometheus queries that will become queryable once Prometheus is deployed and scraping proxy metrics. |
| LP-0MQ1GQ73O008PTBF | RCA: 500 Internal Server Errors from proxy | completed | **Context** — The RCA identified gaps in monitoring coverage; this deployment is a direct response to the need for operational observability infrastructure. |

### Repository files

| File | Relevance |
|------|-----------|
| `monitoring/grafana_llama_memory_dashboard.json` | **Primary target** — Grafana dashboard that will be imported and rendered with live Prometheus data. |
| `monitoring/llama_memory_alerts.yaml` | **Primary target** — Alert rule file that will be loaded into Prometheus for evaluation. |
| `monitoring/proxy_5xx_alerts.yaml` | **Primary target** — Alert rule file that will be loaded into Prometheus for evaluation. |
| `proxy/README.md` (~lines 1128–1170) | **Target documentation** — Contains the example Prometheus scrape config and Grafana reference; will be updated with deployment instructions. |
| `docs/systemd/llama-server.service` | **Pattern** — Systemd user service template that the Prometheus and Grafana service units will follow. |
| `docs/systemd/llama-proxy.service` | **Pattern** — Systemd user service template that the Prometheus and Grafana service units will follow, including log directory patterns, restart policy, and setup commands. |
| `proxy/proxy/metrics.py` | **Reference** — The metrics module that exposes the `/metrics` endpoint Prometheus will scrape. |
data.title: Deploy Prometheus and Grafana instances for monitoring infrastructure

# Slot thrashing reproduction & evidence (test/verification)

Parent: Improve slot management (LP-0MQR0780Z006TLX6)

## Summary
Produce reproducible one-off evidence confirming/refuting inter-session slot stealing between turns and its cache-rebuild cost, committed under `docs/`. This is the verification slice and is created first (test-first ordering).

## Acceptance Criteria
- A runnable one-off repro script reproduces the race: ≥2 simulated concurrent sessions through the proxy with `pool_size=1`, demonstrating a window between response-sent and next-request-arrived where another session acquires the slot.
- Captured output (logs + timing) shows whether a stolen slot triggers a cache rebuild / full reprocess, and measures slot save/restore latency.
- Evidence either confirms or explicitly refutes the AC #1 scenario; if reproduction is inconclusive, minimum-reproduction criteria are documented and worst-case analysis is stated.
- Findings are reproducible by any stakeholder from the committed artifacts (no live-GPU dependency beyond running the script).

## Minimal Implementation
- One-off repro script (e.g. `docs/dev/slot-thrashing-investigation/repro.py`) driving ≥2 sessions against the proxy; reuse machinery/imports from `proxy/tests/test_slot_polling.py` where helpful (NOT added to the permanent test suite).
- Add temporary logging/instrumentation around `SlotLockCoordinator` acquire/release (`proxy/proxy/session.py:579`) and slot save/restore to capture gap timing; document where added so it can be removed.
- Commit captured logs + a short `findings.md` under `docs/dev/`.
- Note coordination with related open items touching slot code paths: Prompt-cache / session reuse tests & small fixes (LP-0MQMC4MKY006J08E), Cleanup: slot-cache retention & cleanup script (LP-0MQMC4MNU002QJK4).

## Dependencies
- None (first slice).

## Deliverables
- Repro script, captured logs/timing, `findings.md`, list of instrumentation touch points.

## Appendix: Clarifying Questions & Answers
- Q4: Is the thrashing-verification feature expected to ship a reusable test harness, or one-off reproducible evidence? — Answer (user): B) One-off reproducible evidence (script + captured logs committed under `docs/`, no permanent test). Source: interactive reply. Final: B.
- Q5: Where should the design document live? — Answer (user): A) New file under `docs/` (e.g. `docs/dev/`). Source: interactive reply. Final: A.
- Q6: Should the recommended approach be prototyped/spiked within this epic? — Answer (user): A) No prototype — paper design only; implementation deferred. Source: interactive reply. Final: A.
data.title: Slot thrashing reproduction & evidence

# Slot management design proposal (implementation/design)

Parent: Improve slot management (LP-0MQR0780Z006TLX6)
Depends on: Slot thrashing reproduction & evidence (LP-0MQR75QYM001HAUB)

## Summary
Author `docs/dev/slot-management-design.md` presenting ≥2 approaches with pros/cons and a clear recommendation grounded in F1 evidence.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Document presents **at least two** distinct approaches with pros/cons covering implementation complexity, cache preservation, fairness across sessions, failure modes/edge cases, and compatibility with existing session machinery (single-flight, delta ingestion, slot save/restore, session header resolution).
- States a clear **recommended approach** with rationale for the 1-GPU / `pool_size=1` / 1–8 agent caseload (work item AC #3).
- Includes **session-end detection analysis** (timeout / explicit signal / heuristic) addressing work item AC #4.
- Includes a **pool_size > 1 viability assessment** (whether multiple save/restore contexts help despite GPU inference serialization).
- Each approach carries a complexity/effort estimate.

## Minimal Implementation
- Create `docs/dev/slot-management-design.md`.
- Structure: Problem (cite F1 findings) → Approaches A–D (session reservation, queue-based, client-side backoff, hybrid) pros/cons → Recommendation + rationale → Session-end detection → pool_size analysis → Per-approach effort estimate.
- Reference existing related work: Session Routing Regression Suite (LP-0MPU11WBN004MFQ8), Observability: slot-reject signal verification (LP-0MPYMVEYO0015J5L), open siblings (LP-0MQMC4MKY006J08E, LP-0MQMC4MNU002QJK4).

## Dependencies
- Slot thrashing reproduction & evidence (LP-0MQR75QYM001HAUB) — evidence informs the recommendation.

## Deliverables
- Design doc `docs/dev/slot-management-design.md`, cross-links to related work items.

## Appendix: Clarifying Questions & Answers
- Q4–Q6: see parent (LP-0MQR0780Z006TLX6) appendix. Design lives under `docs/dev/`; no prototype; verification is one-off reproducible evidence.

data.description:
# Intake Brief: Qwen3 is rebuilding the cache on every request (LP-0MQS214BF004Q055)

## Headline

Investigate and fix the regression where Qwen3 rebuilds the KV cache on every request within a session, restoring the previously working slot save/restore behavior lost after recent changes.

---

## Problem Statement

Qwen3 has started rebuilding the KV cache from scratch on every exchange in a session. This was resolved previously (commit `e9c94d8`, LP-0MPPGCH9K003JEL7) by setting `force_full_prompt: true` and `session_slot_pool_size: 1` along with slot save/restore infrastructure. A recent change has caused the problem to return — every request triggers a full prompt reprocess, negating the KV cache savings from slot save/restore and delta routing.

The slot thrashing investigation (LP-0MQR75QYM001HAUB) confirmed that with `pool_size=1` and ≥2 concurrent sessions, the slot steal rate is 95–98%, meaning nearly every turn transition results in a different session acquiring the slot and overwriting the previous session's KV cache.

---

## Users

- **AI agents** (1–8 concurrent, sharing 1 GPU) making multi-turn requests through the proxy. User story: "As an AI agent, I want my session's KV cache to be preserved between turns so my response latency stays low and I don't pay the full prompt processing cost on every turn."
- **Operators/developers** deploying the proxy. User story: "As an operator, I want cache behavior to be stable and predictable so I can plan capacity and diagnose performance regressions."
- **Developer implementing the fix**. User story: "As a developer, I want to understand why the original fix stopped working so I can restore cache reuse and add regression protection."

---

## Acceptance Criteria

1. **Regression cause identified**: The specific change that caused the cache rebuild regression is identified and documented (commit, file, and mechanism).

2. **Cache reuse restored**: Within a single session with no intervening requests from other sessions, KV cache is reused between turns (confirmed via `slot_restore success` log messages and reduced prompt tokens on follow-up requests).

3. **Slot thrashing mitigation**: The 95–98% slot steal rate is reduced to ≤10% for typical workloads (1–8 concurrent agents, pool_size=1 or higher), measured by slot persistence between session turns.

4. **No mid-job preemption**: While a session has an active job (multi-turn conversation), its slot is not preempted by other sessions' requests.

5. **Regression tests pass**: Proxy integration tests covering the cache path (slot save/restore, delta routing, session continuity) pass reliably.

6. **Full project test suite must pass** with the new changes.

7. **All related documentation is updated** to reflect the changes, including code comments, README, and any relevant wiki or docs site entries.

---

## Constraints

- **Single GPU**: Only 1 physical GPU is available, limiting concurrent inference to one at a time. The solution must work within this constraint.
- **No llama-server changes**: The fix must operate at the proxy level; we cannot modify llama-server's slot management or KV cache internals.
- **Existing session machinery intact**: Single-flight coordination, delta ingestion, slot save/restore, and session header resolution must continue to function.
- **Backward compatibility**: Existing client agents that retry on 503 must not break.
- **Pipeline pressure**: Work items for slot management redesign (LP-0MQR0780Z006TLX6), prompt-cache tests (LP-0MQMC4MKY006J08E), and slot-cache cleanup (LP-0MQMC4MNU002QJK4) are in flight — this fix must coordinate with or work alongside those efforts.

---

## Existing State

### Architecture Overview (current)

1. **Config**: `force_full_prompt: true` for Qwen3, `session_slot_pool_size: 1`, `session_slot_timeout_seconds: 3.0`
2. **Slot save/restore**: Proxy saves KV cache snapshots to disk (`slot-cache/` directory) after responses and restores them before the next request in the same session.
3. **SlotLockCoordinator**: An `asyncio.Lock` per slot-id serializes access, but provides no session affinity — the lock is released immediately after response completion.
4. **Session single-flight**: Only one request per session can be inflight at a time.
5. **Delta routing**: `_classify_delta_routing()` in `server.py` decides whether to forward incremental messages or the full prompt. With `force_full_prompt: true`, delta routing is disabled for Qwen3.

### What was the original fix?

Commit `e9c94d8` (LP-0MPPGCH9K003JEL7) added:
- `force_full_prompt: true` for Qwen3 in config.yaml
- `session_slot_pool_size: 1` 
- `_should_force_full_prompt()` helper in server.py
- Slot save/restore infrastructure

### What changed?

Recent commits on the `dev` branch that could have affected cache behavior:
- `80985e1` — **Release session/slot when upstream returns error** (modifies router.py to decrement active queries on error paths). This changes slot lifecycle but only for error responses (≥400).
- `95239da` — **Remove anthropic/openai providers from config.yaml** — config restructured to `providers[]` format. Qwen3 config was converted but `force_full_prompt: true` was preserved.
- `00eab82` — **Extract session coordination from server.py to session.py** — Pure refactor; extracted slot helpers, observability, and coordination classes into session.py.
- `80185aa` — **Extract core proxy routing to router.py** — Proxy-to-local routing moved to router.py with reconstructed slot save/restore calls.
- Various config changes on dev: `session_guardrail_invalidate_on_cutoff: true` → `false`, `session_guardrail_max_runtime_seconds: 120` → `1800`, `backend_retry_attempts: 3` → `8`, `llama_start_script` changed to `podman_start_llama.sh`.

### Slot thrashing evidence (from LP-0MQR75QYM001HAUB)

- `pool_size=1`: 95–98% steal rate
- `pool_size=4`: 40% steal rate
- 1 session (control): 0% steals
- Gap windows avg ~0.03ms (asyncio cooperative scheduling)
- Save+restore overhead adds ~10ms per turn per session

### Design recommendation (from LP-0MQR7AYRA006IU0P)

The slot management design doc recommends **Approach D (Hybrid reservation + queue, 27–42h)** over simpler approaches, with Phase 1 (Core Reservation, 17–26h) as the MVP.

---

## Desired Change

This work item will:
1. **Identify the specific regression** — determine which commit/config change caused cache rebuilds to resume.
2. **Fix or revert the regression** — if the cause is a simple regression, apply a targeted fix.
3. **Implement slot-affinity improvement** — if the root cause is the slot thrashing (95–98% steal rate), implement at minimum the Phase 1 recommendation from the slot management design doc (session-level slot reservation with idle timeout).
4. **Add regression protection** — tests or instrumentation to detect future cache-rebuild regressions.

The work item should coordinate with the ongoing slot management redesign (LP-0MQR0780Z006TLX6) — either by implementing a subset of the recommended approach or by fixing the regression independently if it's a simpler cause.

---

## Related Work

| Item | Title | Status | Relationship |
|------|-------|--------|-------------|
| LP-0MPPGCH9K003JEL7 | Disable delta routing for models that reprocess full prompt | Completed | **Original fix** — added `force_full_prompt: true` for Qwen3; this work item's fix regressed |
| LP-0MPNVX2HT00774XC | Proxy RCA and full stabilization for cache path | Completed | Parent epic that delivered the original cache stability package |
| LP-0MQR0780Z006TLX6 | Improve slot management | In review | Slot management redesign; design recommends hybrid approach; this item should coordinate |
| LP-0MQR75QYM001HAUB | Slot thrashing reproduction & evidence | In review | Confirmed 95-98% slot steal rate; key evidence for this issue |
| LP-0MQR7AYRA006IU0P | Slot management design proposal | In review | Design doc recommending Approach D (hybrid); Phase 1 (reservation) is the MVP |
| LP-0MQPUCOFJ000QIRF | Release session/slot when upstream returns error | On dev | **Potential regression cause** — modified router.py slot lifecycle for error paths |
| LP-0MPN83BD4004TMAG | Fix session-based cache restore not functioning | Completed | Earlier fix for session_id-based cache restore signal detection |
| LP-0MQMC4MKY006J08E | Prompt-cache / session reuse tests & small fixes | Open | Related — session prompt cache preservation tests |
| LP-0MQMC4MNU002QJK4 | Cleanup: slot-cache retention & cleanup script | Open | Related — slot cache file lifecycle management |

### Key Files

- `proxy/config.yaml` — Qwen3 model config (`force_full_prompt: true`, `session_slot_pool_size: 1`)
- `proxy/proxy/router.py` — Slot save/restore calls, recently modified for error path handling
- `proxy/proxy/session.py` — `SlotLockCoordinator`, slot persistence helpers, session coordination
- `proxy/proxy/server.py` — `_classify_delta_routing`, `_should_force_full_prompt`
- `docs/dev/slot-management-design.md` — Full design proposal for slot management improvement
- `docs/dev/slot-thrashing-investigation/findings.md` — Confirmed thrashing evidence

---

## Risks & Assumptions

- **Risk: Scope creep into full slot management redesign.** The slot management redesign (LP-0MQR0780Z006TLX6) proposes a 49–78h effort. This work item could naturally expand into implementing that full design. **Mitigation:** Focus on identifying the regression first. Only implement slot-affinity improvements if the regression is directly caused by thrashing, and scope to Phase 1 (reservation) of the design doc. Record additional features as follow-up work items.

- **Risk: Regression cause may be difficult to pinpoint.** Multiple commits affect the slot/cache path — extraction refactors, config changes, router.py modifications. The cause could be subtle (e.g., an import order issue, a config default change). **Mitigation:** Use `git bisect` between the last-known-good and first-known-bad commits. Add targeted logging if needed.

- **Risk: Fix may conflict with ongoing slot management redesign.** The slot management design work is in review and may recommend changes that conflict with a targeted regression fix. **Mitigation:** Coordinate with LP-0MQR0780Z006TLX6. If the regression fix is simple (e.g., config default), apply it independently. If the fix requires slot-affinity changes, align with the design doc's Phase 1.

- **Risk: The thrashing problem may mask the regression.** The 95–98% slot steal rate means that even without a regression, cache would be rebuilt frequently. The "every request" behavior might be the thrashing, not a regression. **Mitigation:** Test with a single session to isolate whether cache is preserved without contention. Compare behavior before and after the suspected regression commits.

- **Assumption:** The regression is caused by a specific recent commit, not a pre-existing condition that was previously undetected.
- **Assumption:** A single-session test (no contention) can distinguish between a cache regression and the known thrashing problem.
- **Assumption:** The original fix (commit `e9c94d8`) was working correctly before the regression.
- **Risk: Overlap with Improve slot management (LP-0MQR0780Z006TLX6).** This work item's fix may duplicate or conflict with the slot management redesign, which is already in review. **Mitigation:** Identify the regression cause first. If the fix is a simple regression (config, import, or missing call), apply it independently. If the fix requires slot-affinity changes, align with LP-0MQR0780Z006TLX6's Phase 1 scope and document the boundary explicitly.
- **Assumption:** Config defaults on the `dev` branch are correct and `force_full_prompt: true` is still being applied for Qwen3.

---

## Appendix: Clarifying Questions & Answers

- **Q1:** "Is the cache rebuild happening on every request even with a single session (no slot contention), or only under multi-session load?"
  - **Status:** OPEN QUESTION — This distinction determines whether the issue is a regression in cache mechanics vs. the known slot thrashing problem.
  - **Importance:** Critical for scoping. If single-session cache is broken, the fix is in a different code path than if only multi-session thrashing is the problem.

- **Q2:** "Which specific deployment/environment is showing the symptom? (dev branch or main branch?)"
  - **Status:** OPEN QUESTION — The dev branch has significant changes (config restructure, extraction refactors, error-path modifications) not yet on main. The regression may only affect the dev branch.
  - **Importance:** Determines whether the fix targets dev-branch changes or main-branch behavior.

- **Q3:** "Has the symptom been observed on a branch where `force_full_prompt: true` is confirmed active for Qwen3?"
  - **Status:** OPEN QUESTION — If the config restructuring (commit `95239da`) inadvertently dropped or altered the `force_full_prompt` flag, the proxy would try delta routing, which is known to fail for Qwen3.
  - **Importance:** Quick check — verify that the running config still has `force_full_prompt: true` for the Qwen3 model entry.

- **Q4:** "Is the cache rebuild confirmed via proxy logs (e.g., absence of `slot_restore success` messages) or via llama-server logs (e.g., 'forcing full prompt re-processing')?"
  - **Status:** OPEN QUESTION — Different log evidence points to different root causes. Missing proxy-side restore indicates slot lifecycle issues; llama-server forcing reprocess indicates model compatibility issues.
  - **Importance:** Diagnostic — guides the investigation toward the right code path.

## Child Work Items

This work item has been decomposed into 12 implementation work items by the `intakeall` process, organized into 4 phases following the Job-Level Slot Ownership design (see `docs/dev/slot-management-design.md`):

**Phase 1 — Core Scheduler (P1–P3):**

- LP-0MQS6MPFC004E8E0 — admit_job: assign idle slot or enqueue
- LP-0MQS6MPHU000D3CW — reenter_job: fast path for active jobs

**Phase 2 — Queue and Release (P4–P6):**

- LP-0MQS6MPOZ003YS9G — Job timeout background task

**Phase 3 — Router Integration (P7–P9):**
- LP-0MQS6MPRJ0011YRL — Router integration with JobScheduler
- LP-0MQS6MPTY002T402 — Wire scheduler into SlotLockCoordinator
- LP-0MQS6MPWE005NJJA — Session invalidation → slot release

**Phase 4 — Testing and Config (P10–P12):**

- LP-0MQS6MQ180000W0Q — Integration tests (router + scheduler)
- LP-0MQS6MQ3R002G1XL — Configuration keys and documentation

data.description:
## Goal

Add the `JobScheduler` class to manage job-to-slot assignment with ownership semantics.

## Requirements

1. Create `JobScheduler` class in `proxy/slot_scheduler.py`
2. Track per-slot state: `Idle` or `Owned(session_id)`
3. Track active jobs: `dict[session_id, slot_id]`
4. Track slot-to-job mapping: `dict[slot_id, session_id]`
5. Initialize with configurable `pool_size` slots

## Acceptance Criteria
- JobScheduler can be instantiated with pool_size slots
- All slots start in Idle state
- Active jobs and slot-to-job mappings are empty
- Unit tests verify state initialization

## Design Reference
See `docs/dev/slot-management-design.md` §4.1
data.title: Phase 1: Core JobScheduler - slot state tracking

data.description:
## Goal

Implement `admit_job()` — assign idle slot or enqueue waiting job.

## Requirements

1. Implement `admit_job(session_id) -> AdmitResult` method:
   - If job already owns a slot → return ASSIGNED(slot_id)
   - If idle slot available → assign it, return ASSIGNED(slot_id)
   - If all slots busy and queue not full → enqueue, return QUEUED
   - If queue full → return REJECTED_503(retry_after=N)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Job re-entry returns existing slot
- Idle slot assignment works correctly
- Queue overflow returns 503 with retry_after
- Unit tests for all admission paths

## Design Reference
See `docs/dev/slot-management-design.md` §4.2
data.title: Phase 1: admit_job - assign idle slot or enqueue

data.description:
## Goal

Implement `reenter_job()` — fast path for active jobs (same slot every request).

## Requirements

1. Implement `reenter_job(session_id) -> Optional[int]` method:
   - If job is active → return slot_id
   - If job not active → return None (caller must call admit_job())

2. This is the critical performance path — all subsequent requests from an active job must hit this fast path.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Active jobs always get same slot_id
- Inactive jobs return None
- Method completes in <1ms (no locking overhead)
- Unit tests for active and inactive job paths

## Design Reference
See `docs/dev/slot-management-design.md` §4.4
data.title: Phase 1: reenter_job - fast path for active jobs

data.description:
## Goal

Implement queue overflow rejection with Retry-After header.

## Requirements

1. Queue max_depth configurable via `slot_queue_max_depth` (default: 16)
2. When queue is full → return 503 with Retry-After header
3. Retry-After value configurable via `slot_queue_overflow_retry_after` (default: 900s = 15 minutes)
4. FIFO ordering — first job enqueued is first dequeued

## Acceptance Criteria
- Queue respects max_depth
- Overflow returns 503 with correct Retry-After header
- FIFO ordering verified
- Integration test with queue overflow scenario

## Design Reference
See `docs/dev/slot-management-design.md` §3.3
data.title: Phase 2: Queue overflow rejection with Retry-After

data.description:
## Goal

Implement `release_slot()` — on job completion, slot → Idle → assign next queued job.

## Requirements

1. Implement `release_slot(slot_id)` method:
   - Clear slot ownership (state → Idle)
   - Remove job from active_jobs mapping
   - If queue not empty → assign next job from queue to this slot
   - If queue empty → slot stays Idle

2. Called on: session invalidation, session timeout, job completion

## Acceptance Criteria
- Slot returns to Idle state after release
- Next queued job is assigned immediately
- Active job mapping is cleaned up
- Integration test: job completes → slot released → next job assigned

## Design Reference
See `docs/dev/slot-management-design.md` §4.3
data.title: Phase 2: release_slot - slot release and next job assignment

data.description:
## Goal

Add job timeout background task — release idle jobs after timeout period.

## Requirements

1. Background task runs every 10s to check for idle jobs
2. If a job has been idle (no requests) for longer than `slot_job_timeout_seconds` (default: 300s = 5min), release its slot
3. Timeout check must not block the main event loop
4. Log timeout events at WARNING level

## Acceptance Criteria
- Idle jobs are released after timeout
- Background task runs every 10s
- Timeout does not block main event loop
- Timeout events logged at WARNING level
- Unit test for timeout behavior

## Design Reference
See `docs/dev/slot-management-design.md` §4.5
data.title: Phase 2: Job timeout background task

data.description:
## Goal

Update `router.py` to use the JobScheduler for slot assignment.

## Requirements

1. In `proxy_to_local()`, replace direct slot lock acquisition with scheduler calls:
   - First try `reenter_job(session_id)` for fast path
   - If None, call `admit_job(session_id)` for new job admission
   - If QUEUED or REJECTED, return 503 with Retry-After

2. The scheduler decides which slot a job gets — router just uses the assigned slot_id

3. On session end/timeout, call `release_slot(slot_id)` to free the slot

## Acceptance Criteria
- Active jobs always go to their assigned slot
- New jobs are admitted to idle slots or queued
- Queued jobs get 503 with Retry-After
- Session invalidation releases the slot
- Integration tests with router

## Design Reference
See `docs/dev/slot-management-design.md` §5
data.title: Phase 3: Router integration with JobScheduler

data.description:
## Goal

Wire the JobScheduler into SlotLockCoordinator so they work together.

## Requirements

1. Add `set_scheduler(scheduler)` method to SlotLockCoordinator
2. On startup, create JobScheduler and inject it into SlotLockCoordinator
3. SlotLockCoordinator.acquire() now works with scheduler-assigned slots
4. Ensure backward compatibility — existing code that uses SlotLockCoordinator still works

## Acceptance Criteria
- Scheduler is injected into SlotLockCoordinator at startup
- SlotLockCoordinator.acquire() works with scheduler-assigned slots
- No regressions in existing behavior
- Integration tests

## Design Reference
See `docs/dev/slot-management-design.md` §5.2
data.title: Phase 3: Wire scheduler into SlotLockCoordinator

data.description:
## Goal

Handle session invalidation → slot release.

## Requirements

1. When a session is invalidated (end of conversation, guardrail cutoff), the scheduler must release the job's slot
2. This hook should be called from the existing session invalidation path in `router.py`
3. Slot release triggers the next queued job to be assigned

## Acceptance Criteria
- Session invalidation releases the slot
- Next queued job is assigned immediately after release
- No orphaned slot ownership (slot → Idle when job ends)
- Integration test: invalidate session → slot released → next job assigned

## Design Reference
See `docs/dev/slot-management-design.md` §5.3

## Goal

Run git bisect between last-known-good (e9c94d8) and current dev HEAD to confirm whether any code regression beyond slot thrashing is causing the Qwen3 cache rebuild behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. git bisect run completed between e9c94d8 and current dev HEAD
2. Single-session test harness used to isolate cache rebuild vs. thrashing
3. Findings documented in work item comment with commit hash and mechanism
4. If no regression found beyond thrashing: mark resolved, no further action
5. If regression found: document the responsible commit and add it to the parent epic

## Rationale

The slot thrashing evidence (F1) already confirms 95-98% steal rate as the primary cause. This spike exists to rule out any additional code regression beyond thrashing.
