data.description:
## Summary
Requests fail with fallback flow after initial provider response `503 Model plan is loading, retry shortly`.

Observed behavior from operator:
1. First request returned: `Error: 503 Model plan is loading, retry shortly`.
2. After logs indicated model was loaded, retrying the same request returned: `Error: 503 All providers exhausted`.
3. Separate client request directly to Qwen3 succeeded.
4. Cache prefill was running before the failures.

This suggests fallback/provider-health state is not recovering correctly after transient model-loading errors, or cache prefill side-effects are incorrectly marking providers unavailable.

## User Story
As an API client, when one provider returns a transient model-loading 503, fallback should recover and route to an available provider once loaded, instead of remaining stuck in an exhausted state.

## Expected Behavior
- Transient model-loading responses should not permanently exhaust provider selection.
- Retried requests after model load should succeed if any configured provider is healthy.
- Cache prefill activity should not poison provider health for normal request handling.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A regression test reproduces the sequence: initial model-loading 503 followed by successful retry/fallback once provider is available.
- [ ] Root cause in fallback/provider-health/cache-prefill interaction is fixed.
- [ ] Existing and new tests pass locally.
- [ ] Work item comments include summary of changed files and commit hash.
data.title: Fallback not recovering after model-loading 503; returns all providers exhausted

data.description:
## Summary

When a query falls back to a remote provider (free tier) that responds slowly, the Pi client times out and sends retry HTTP requests. Each retry creates a new session, a new local retry loop, and eventually a new remote fallback call — multiplying contention for the single Qwen3 slot and overloading the remote provider.

## Impact

2 user queries generated 4+ HTTP requests, 3+ sessions, and 2+ remote provider calls, all competing for the same resources. This causes a cascade: slow remote → Pi retry → more local contention → more fallbacks → even slower.

## Reproduction

1. Send 2 simultaneous queries to model `plan` via Pi
2. First query takes the single Qwen3 slot
3. Second query enters `proxy_with_fallback` → local slot busy → 2 local retries (with 0.25s delay each) → falls back to remote free tier
4. Pi client has a timeout shorter than the fallback latency → retries the request
5. Retry also enters `proxy_with_fallback` → same pattern
6. Multiple concurrent fallback calls to the same remote provider

## Root Cause Analysis

- `proxy_with_fallback` has `local_slot_exhaustion_retry_attempts: 2` with `local_slot_exhaustion_retry_delay_seconds: 0.25` — the retries add \~0.5s of delay before falling back, during which Pi may time out
- Pi appears to have a per-request timeout that fires before the fallback completes
- No deduplication or coalescing of identical in-flight requests
- Each retry gets a new session because Pi sends no session header (`session={}` in logs)

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Two simultaneous Pi queries to `plan` should not generate more than 2 proxy request handlers
2. Retry requests from Pi for the same logical query should be coalesced or the fallback should complete within Pi's timeout window
3. Number of concurrent remote fallback calls should not exceed the number of distinct user queries
4. All existing tests still pass
data.title: Pi retry cascade creates duplicate queries when fallback is slow

data.description:
When `proxy_with_fallback` reaches the `opencode-go` provider (third in the `plan` chain), the call fails within ~400ms and the provider enters 60s cooldown — yet calling the same Go endpoint directly with the same key returns HTTP 200. This collapses the fallback chain, surfacing "All providers exhausted" while the Go tier is reachable and authenticated.

## Problem Statement

The Go tier (`opencode-go`) is the paid fallback that should activate when the free tier is rate-limited. When it fails through the proxy, the fallback chain collapses and users are unable to complete requests even though the Go endpoint is directly reachable and authenticated.

## Users

- **Proxy operators** running llama-proxy with `plan` model configuration that includes `opencode-go` as a fallback provider
- **End users** whose requests fall through to remote providers when local Qwen3 slots are saturated and the free tier is rate-limited

### User Stories

- As a proxy operator, when my local Qwen3 slots are saturated and the free remote tier returns 429, the Go tier should serve the request successfully and transparently, so that users never see "All providers exhausted" when a paid tier is available.
- As a developer debugging the fallback chain, when `proxy_with_fallback` routes to `opencode-go`, the model name in the forwarded body should be correctly overridden to `deepseek-v4-flash` and the API key should be injected from Pi's `auth.json`, so that the Go endpoint receives valid input.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. A request through the proxy to `plan` that reaches the `opencode-go` provider returns HTTP 200 with valid content
2. The provider name `opencode-go` correctly resolves the API key from Pi's `auth.json`
3. The `model` field in the forwarded body is overridden from `plan` to `deepseek-v4-flash` (the Go tier model)
4. A specific regression test verifies `proxy_to_remote` with `opencode-go` provider config succeeds
5. All existing tests still pass
6. Full project test suite must pass with the new changes
7. All related documentation is updated to reflect the changes, including code comments, README, and any relevant wiki or docs site entries

## Constraints

- The solution must not change the behavior of other providers in the fallback chain (local-qwen3, opencode-deepseek-free)
- The solution must handle both `api_key_env` and Pi `auth.json` key resolution paths
- Must not introduce additional latency on the successful path
- The bug is intermittent; regression tests must reliably reproduce the scenario

## Existing State

The `plan` model config in `proxy/config.yaml` has three ordered providers:
1. `local-qwen3` (type: local)
2. `opencode-deepseek-free` (type: remote, endpoint `https://opencode.ai/zen`, model `deepseek-v4-flash-free`)
3. `opencode-go` (type: remote, endpoint `https://opencode.ai/zen/go`, model `deepseek-v4-flash`)

The `opencode-go` provider does **not** specify `api_key_env` in its config — unlike `opencode-deepseek-free` which sets `api_key_env: OPENCODE_API_KEY`. This means the `opencode-go` provider relies entirely on the `_try_pi_auth_json("opencode-go")` fallback in `proxy/proxy/proxy_remote.py` for key resolution.

The `_try_pi_auth_json` function looks up keys case-insensitively from `~/.pi/agent/auth.json`.

## Desired Change

1. **Diagnose** whether the `model` override works correctly when `proxy_with_fallback` calls `proxy_to_remote` with the `opencode-go` provider config. The `proxy_to_remote` function reads `model_config.get("model")` to set the upstream model — verify the chain passes the correct provider config dict.
2. **Diagnose** whether `_try_pi_auth_json("opencode-go")` is called and returns a valid key. The key resolution in `proxy_to_remote` checks `api_key_env` first, then `api_key` config field, then falls back to `_try_pi_auth_json(provider_name)`.
3. **Fix** whichever root cause is identified (model name forwarding, key injection, or both).
4. **Add** a regression test that simulates the fallback reaching `opencode-go` and verifies the request succeeds.

## Key Files

- `proxy/proxy/proxy_remote.py` — Contains `proxy_to_remote()` which forwards requests to remote API endpoints and `_try_pi_auth_json()` which falls back to Pi's auth.json for credentials. Most likely location of the bug (model name override or missing auth header).
- `proxy/proxy/provider.py` — Contains `proxy_with_fallback()` which iterates through the provider chain and calls `proxy_to_remote` for each remote provider. Must verify the correct provider config dict is passed.
- `proxy/config.yaml` — Defines the `plan` model with its three providers (local-qwen3, opencode-deepseek-free, opencode-go). `opencode-go` is missing `api_key_env`.
- `proxy/tests/test_provider_fallback.py` — Unit tests for fallback logic; new regression tests should be added here.
- `proxy/tests/test_plan_fallback_live_e2e.py` — Live E2E integration tests; may be extended to cover the Go tier fallback path.

## Related Work

- **LP-0MR1F9SB3003SKU9** (Pi retry cascade creates duplicate queries when fallback is slow) — The duplicate queries from the retry cascade may exacerbate the Go tier failure by creating concurrent calls to the same provider.
- **LP-0MR01SJBP005I0OR** (Fallback not recovering after model-loading 503; returns all providers exhausted) — Previously completed work that fixed related fallback issues including streaming response handling, header normalization, and local provider cooldown behavior. Provides context on `proxy_with_fallback` architecture.

## Risks & Assumptions

### Assumptions
- The `opencode-go` endpoint (`https://opencode.ai/zen/go`) accepts the `deepseek-v4-flash` model ID and the API key from `auth.json` — this is confirmed by direct test.
- The bug is in the proxy's forwarding logic rather than in the Go endpoint itself.
- The issue is reproducible under specific conditions (local saturation + free tier rate-limited).

### Risks
- **Scope creep**: Opportunities to improve provider config validation or consolidate key resolution logic could emerge during diagnosis. These should be recorded as separate work items rather than inflating the scope of this bug fix.
- **Intermittent reproduction**: The bug was observed once and is not consistently reproducible, making diagnosis challenging. The fix must include reliable regression tests.
- **Auth dependency**: If `_try_pi_auth_json` is the correct intended key path for `opencode-go`, the fix is minimal. If the config should also include `api_key_env` (like `opencode-deepseek-free`), the design decision is a config change vs. code change.

## Appendix: Clarifying Questions & Answers

No interview was required — the work item was sufficiently well-defined from the initial submission. The existing description includes:
- A clear problem summary and impact statement
- Reproduction steps (intermittent)
- Three suspected root causes with specific code references
- Five measurable acceptance criteria
- A related work item reference
data.title: Go tier (opencode-go) fails through proxy fallback despite working when called directly

data.description:
# Summary

The proxy's stream_generator in proxy_remote.py doesn't catch httpx.ReadTimeout exceptions that occur during response.aiter_bytes(). When a read timeout happens, the exception bubbles up unhandled, causing an HTTP 500 error instead of graceful degradation (logging + final SSE event).

## Error

httpcore.ReadTimeout -> httpx.ReadTimeout -> at proxy/proxy/proxy_remote.py:314 in stream_generator

## Fix

Add a catch block for httpx.ReadTimeout in stream_generator that:
1. Logs a WARNING about the timeout
2. Yields a final SSE event (if the client hasn't disconnected and upstream didn't send a finish marker)
3. Proceeds to the finally block for cleanup

## Acceptance criteria

1. httpx.ReadTimeout is caught in stream_generator and handled gracefully (no 500 error)
2. A WARNING-level log is emitted when the timeout occurs
3. A final SSE event is sent (if not already completed) to signal completion
4. The finally block cleanup still runs after the timeout
5. All existing tests pass
data.title: Handle httpx.ReadTimeout in streaming proxy path gracefully

# Implement: model cache cleanup script

## Summary
Create `scripts/cleanup-model-cache.sh` that dynamically reads `models.ini` and removes stale HuggingFace model caches and the duplicate llama.cpp GGUF cache.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Script `scripts/cleanup-model-cache.sh` is created and marked executable.
2. Script dynamically reads all model presets from `models.ini` (every `[section]` with `hf-repo`).
3. Script removes HuggingFace cached models NOT in the keep list from `~/.cache/huggingface/hub/`.
4. Script removes the llama.cpp GGUF duplicate cache file (`~/.cache/llama.cpp/ggml-org_gemma-4-31B-it-GGUF_gemma-4-31B-it-Q8_0.gguf`).
5. `--dry-run` flag previews deletions without removing anything.
6. Script is idempotent: running multiple times produces no side effects after first run.
7. Stale models removed total ~200 GB: gpt-oss-120b (two providers, 119 GB), Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct (three variants, 63 GB), DeepSeek-Coder-V2-Lite-Instruct (16 GB).
8. Script validates `models.ini` parses correctly and has at least one `hf-repo` entry before proceeding.
9. Script logs actions to stdout for cron mail capture.

## Minimal Implementation

- Create `scripts/cleanup-model-cache.sh`:
  - Parse `models.ini` with grep/awk to extract `[section]` names and `hf-repo` values.
  - Scan `~/.cache/huggingface/hub/models--*` directories; compare against configured models.
  - Use `podman image inspect` or manual directory listing to identify stale models.
  - Remove duplicate llama.cpp GGUF cache.
  - Support `--dry-run` flag.
- Create tests in `tests/test_cleanup_model_cache.sh` (sibling work item).
- Add script to cron via `cleanup-all.sh` (sibling work item).

## Constraints

- POSIX shell (bash) only -- no Python or other runtime dependencies.
- Must respect HuggingFace cache directory structure (`~/.cache/huggingface/hub/models--*`).
- Must not interfere with active model serving or container operations.

## Deliverables

- `scripts/cleanup-model-cache.sh`
- `tests/test_cleanup_model_cache.sh` (created by sibling test item)
- Cron entry via `cleanup-all.sh`

## Related Work

- blocked-by: LP-0MR4X9FVO007ZRUV (Add Qwen3-Coder-Next to models.ini) -- script needs ini updated first
- blocked-by: LP-0MR4X9FVO007ZRUV (Test: model cache cleanup script) -- implementation depends on test definition
- related-to: LP-0MQMC4MNU002QJK4 (slot-cache cleanup -- similar script pattern and cron integration)
- related-to: LP-0MNXARX6A0045GJO (gemma4 default -- made gpt-oss stale)
data.title: Implement: model cache cleanup script

# Test: model cache cleanup script

## Summary
Write test cases for `cleanup-model-cache.sh` to validate correctness before implementation.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Test: `--dry-run` flag outputs candidate files without deleting anything.
2. Test: script is idempotent -- running twice produces identical output on the second run.
3. Test: script correctly parses `models.ini` and identifies all configured models.
4. Test: script correctly identifies stale models not in `models.ini` (gpt-oss, Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B, DeepSeek-Coder-V2).
5. Test: script identifies and flags the duplicate llama.cpp GGUF cache file.
6. Test: script handles a missing or malformed `models.ini` gracefully (logs error, does not delete anything).
7. Test: script exits 0 on success, non-zero only on unexpected errors.

## Minimal Implementation

- Create a test script `tests/test_cleanup_model_cache.sh` that:
  - Sets up a temporary `models.ini` and mock cache directories.
  - Runs the cleanup script in dry-run mode and validates output.
  - Verifies idempotency by running twice and comparing results.
  - Tests edge cases (missing file, empty sections, no hf-repo fields).
- Run the test suite to confirm all tests pass.

## Constraints

- Tests must use only POSIX shell and standard tools (no external test framework).
- Tests should create temporary directories and clean them up afterward.
- Tests must not operate on the real `~/.cache/huggingface/hub` directory.

## Deliverables

- `tests/test_cleanup_model_cache.sh` with all test cases passing.

## Related Work

- blocked-by: LP-0MR4X9FVO007ZRUV (Add Qwen3-Coder-Next to models.ini) -- script depends on ini having all models defined
- related-to: LP-0MQMC4MNU002QJK4 (slot-cache cleanup -- similar script pattern)

data.description:
# Intake Brief: Increase llama_request_timeout to 25 minutes

## Problem Statement

The proxy's `llama_request_timeout` is set to 300 seconds (5 minutes), but the local Qwen3 model processes prompts of 65K–93K tokens that take 10–19 minutes just for prompt evaluation. This causes the proxy to cancel connections before the model can generate any output, triggering cascading fallback failures to remote providers.

## Users

- **Operators running multiple agents through the proxy**: Need agents to receive responses instead of timeouts when processing large prompts.
- **Developer agents (e.g., pi-agent)**: Submit large prompts (65K+ tokens) that exceed the 5-minute timeout during prompt evaluation.

### User Stories

- As an operator running multiple agents through the proxy, I want the request timeout to be long enough to accommodate large prompts so that agents receive responses instead of timeouts.
- As a developer agent, I want my large prompts to be fully processed without premature connection cancellation so that I can complete my tasks.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `proxy/config.yaml` has `llama_request_timeout: 1500` (25 minutes)
2. A request with a large prompt (20K+ tokens) completes without timeout errors
3. Existing tests still pass
4. The proxy health endpoint remains responsive during long-running requests
5. All related documentation is updated to reflect the changes, including code comments, README, and any relevant wiki or docs site entries.
6. Full project test suite must pass with the new changes.

## Constraints

- The timeout value must be sufficient for 65K–93K token prompts (observed processing time: 10–19 minutes)
- The timeout should not be so large that genuinely stuck requests consume resources indefinitely (mitigated by `session_guardrail_max_runtime_seconds: 1800`)
- The adaptive timeout (`llama_adaptive_timeout_enabled: true`) already scales with prompt size; this change raises the hard ceiling

## Existing State

- `proxy/config.yaml` currently has `llama_request_timeout: 300` (5 minutes) — **already updated to 1500 in commit 819d6c0**
- llama-server logs show prompt processing times of 49–1,145 seconds for prompts of 16K–93K tokens
- Connections are canceled with `Connection handling canceled` before token generation begins
- Related completed work: LP-0MQ5QVSMX007C5S4 (adaptive timeout), LP-0MPHM16H9002O54V (proxy timeouts), LP-0MQ5FNQ9I006N4PO (provider fallback)

## Desired Change

- Update `llama_request_timeout` in `proxy/config.yaml` from 300 to 1500 (25 minutes)
- Verify the proxy handles long-running requests without connection errors
- **Status: Already implemented in commit 819d6c0**

## Key Files

- `proxy/config.yaml` — Contains `llama_request_timeout` setting (changed from 300 to 1500)
- `proxy/proxy/router_helpers.py` — Contains `_compute_request_timeout()` which reads the timeout value
- `proxy/proxy/router.py` — Uses the timeout when making backend requests
- `proxy/proxy/proxy_remote.py` — Uses the timeout for remote provider requests
- `proxy/tests/test_provider_fallback.py` — Tests that reference `llama_request_timeout`
- `proxy/tests/test_backend_resilience.py` — Tests that reference `llama_request_timeout`

## Related Work

- **LP-0MQ5QVSMX007C5S4**: "Improve proxy backend resilience: increase retries and adaptive timeouts" (completed) — Introduced adaptive timeout that scales with prompt size; this change raises the hard ceiling
- **LP-0MPHM16H9002O54V**: "Add proxy timeouts and concurrency limits" (completed) — Original timeout infrastructure
- **LP-0MQ5FNQ9I006N4PO**: "Provider fallback for model requests" (completed) — Uses `llama_request_timeout` for each provider attempt
- **LP-0MR5I2I6T0039T06**: "Enable guardrail cutoff to kill stuck sessions" (in-progress) — Committed in same commit (819d6c0); enables `session_guardrail_invalidate_on_cutoff: true`

## Appendix: Clarifying Questions & Answers

- **Q**: "Is the timeout value of 25 minutes (1500 seconds) sufficient for 65K–93K token prompts?" — Answer (user/stakeholder): Yes, the observed processing time is 10–19 minutes for these prompts, so 25 minutes provides adequate headroom. Source: llama-server logs showing 49–1,145 seconds for 16K–93K tokens. Final: yes.
- **Q**: "Should we also enable session guardrail cutoff?" — Answer (user/stakeholder): Yes, this was bundled into the same commit (819d6c0) as LP-0MR5I2I6T0039T06. Setting `session_guardrail_invalidate_on_cutoff: true` ensures genuinely stuck sessions are killed. Final: yes.

## Risk & Scope Notes

- **Risk**: A 25-minute timeout could allow genuinely stuck requests to consume resources. **Mitigation**: The `session_guardrail_max_runtime_seconds: 1800` (30 minutes) provides a hard ceiling, and `session_guardrail_invalidate_on_cutoff: true` (now enabled) kills stuck sessions.
- **Scope note**: Any additional features or refactorings discovered during implementation should be recorded as work items linked to this item, rather than expanding scope.
data.title: Increase llama_request_timeout to 25 minutes

data.description:
## Summary

The proxy's session guardrails for cutoff and repetition are disabled (`session_guardrail_invalidate_on_cutoff: false`, `session_guardrail_invalidate_on_repetition: false`). When a session gets stuck (e.g., due to timeout fallback loops or model issues), it can run for up to 30 minutes (`session_guardrail_max_runtime_seconds: 1800`) without being killed, consuming resources and blocking other agents.

## User Story

As an operator running multiple agents through the proxy, I want stuck sessions to be automatically killed so that resources are freed up for other agents.

## Current State

- `proxy/config.yaml` has `session_guardrail_invalidate_on_cutoff: false`
- `proxy/config.yaml` has `session_guardrail_invalidate_on_repetition: false`
- Stuck sessions can run for up to 30 minutes
- The proxy log shows streams sending heartbeat keepalives (`:`) for 14+ minutes without producing output

## Desired Change

- Set `session_guardrail_invalidate_on_cutoff: true` in `proxy/config.yaml`
- Keep `session_guardrail_invalidate_on_repetition: false` (repetition may be valid for some use cases)
- Consider reducing `session_guardrail_max_runtime_seconds` from 1800 to 600 (10 minutes) as a safety net

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `proxy/config.yaml` has `session_guardrail_invalidate_on_cutoff: true`
2. A session that exceeds `session_guardrail_max_runtime_seconds` is killed and the slot is freed
3. The session invalidation is logged with the reason
4. Existing tests still pass
data.title: Enable guardrail cutoff to kill stuck sessions

data.description:
# Intake Brief: Investigate and reduce agent prompt size inflation

## Problem Statement

Pi coding agents are constructing prompts of 65K–93K tokens when sending requests to the local Qwen3 model through the proxy. At ~81 tokens/sec prompt processing, this translates to 13–19 minutes of prompt evaluation time per request, causing timeout cascades and blocking concurrent agent sessions. The root cause of the large prompts lies in how the pi agent framework constructs its prompts — not in the proxy forwarding layer (which already supports session-based delta ingestion and slot management).

## Users

- **Operators** running pi coding agents through the local proxy, who need prompt processing to complete in seconds (not minutes) so multiple agents can work concurrently.
- **pi agent developers** who need to understand which components of the agent prompt system (system prompts, tool definitions, context history, skill content) contribute most to prompt size inflation.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. A work item comment documenting the composition of typical pi agent prompts, including: system prompt size, context/cache accumulation, tool definition size, skill content size, and any duplicated content across turns.
2. Identification of the top 3 sources of prompt size inflation with evidence (e.g., percentage of total token count or absolute token count per source).
3. Recommendations for reducing prompt size (e.g., context truncation strategies, tool definition deduplication, system prompt optimization, skill content pruning) — recommendations only, not implementation.
4. A target maximum prompt size (e.g., 32K tokens) documented in `proxy/README.md`.
5. All related documentation is updated to reflect the changes, including code comments, README, and any relevant docs entries.
6. Full project test suite must pass with the new changes.

## Constraints

- The pi agent framework is at `~/.pi/agent/` and its prompt construction logic is not part of this repo; investigation must work with available data (proxy logs, session files, agent configuration).
- Prompt size reduction recommendations must not break agent functionality (e.g., tool definitions and system prompts exist for a reason).
- The proxy layer (`proxy/`) is the integration point — final target size documentation goes in `proxy/README.md`.

## Existing State

- **llama-server logs** show prompts of 65K–93K tokens arriving at the backend.
- **Prompt processing** at ~81 tokens/sec on the local machine: 13–19 minutes for prompt eval.
- **Prompt cache** is thrashing (8GB limit, evicting entries frequently).
- **Slot configuration** changed from `session_slot_pool_size: 1` to slot management with `slot_pool_size: 4`, but agent-side prompt sizes remain large.
- **Proxy metrics** show `sessions_active: 26`, `restore_success_total: 0` — delta ingestion is not reducing prompt sizes as expected.
- **Pi agent configuration**: Uses "Local Proxy" provider at `http://192.168.0.199:8000/v1` with models `qwen3`, `plan`, `code`, `gpt120`.
- **Pi session data** available in `~/.pi/agent/sessions/` includes hundreds of session JSONL files spanning April–July 2026.
- **Related work** (all completed, proxy-side):
  - LP-0MQS214BF004Q055 — "Qwen3 is rebuilding the cache on every request" (slot thrashing fix via JobScheduler)
  - LP-0MO6DJP0P006WCHA — "Implement incremental session-based prompt ingestion" (delta ingestion)
  - LP-0MPN83BD4004TMAG — "Fix session-based cache restore not functioning" (session restore signal fix)
  - LP-0MPNVNL0W008Z0L4 — "Verify context cache behavior from live proxy logs" (cache verification)
  - LP-0MPNTJ165004M5GX — "Investigate high CPU usage in llama-server" (CPU root cause analysis)

## Desired Change

The investigation will:
1. Sample actual pi agent requests sent through the proxy (from proxy logs and pi session files in `~/.pi/agent/sessions/`).
2. Analyze prompt composition: tokenize messages, categorize by role (system, user, assistant), measure tool definition bloat, skill content, and accumulated context.
3. Identify the top 3 sources of inflation with concrete evidence.
4. Propose specific recommendations for reducing each source of inflation.
5. Propose and document a target maximum prompt size in `proxy/README.md`.
6. Publish findings as a work item comment on LP-0MR5I2OK9007H5NU.

**Key Files:**

- `proxy/proxy/lifecycle.py` — Contains `_estimate_prompt_tokens()` for token estimation from request bodies; may need to be extended or called during investigation.
- `proxy/proxy/server.py` — Main proxy server handling incoming requests; could be instrumented to log prompt composition breakdown.
- `proxy/proxy/session_manager.py` — Manages session history; relevant for understanding per-turn context accumulation.
- `proxy/proxy/router.py` — Routes requests; may need to capture prompt size metrics.
- `proxy/proxy/metrics.py` — Existing metrics infrastructure; could be extended to capture prompt-size distributions.
- `proxy/README.md` — Target for documenting the recommended maximum prompt size (AC #4).
- `~/.pi/agent/sessions/` — Pi session files containing the actual prompts sent to the proxy.
- `~/.pi/agent/prompts/` — Pi agent prompt templates (intake.md, plan.md, review.md, etc.) that contribute to prompt composition.
- `~/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md` — Core agent instructions that may be included in every prompt.

## Related Work

### Related Work Items

- **LP-0MQS214BF004Q055** "Qwen3 is rebuilding the cache on every request" (completed, critical) — Addressed slot thrashing by implementing JobScheduler with slot ownership. Proxy-side fix; agent-side prompt inflation remained unaddressed.
- **LP-0MO6DJP0P006WCHA** "Implement incremental session-based prompt ingestion" (completed, critical) — Added session-based delta ingestion for proxy-to-llama-server forwarding. Agent still sends full prompts each time.
- **LP-0MPN83BD4004TMAG** "Fix session-based cache restore not functioning" (completed, critical) — Fixed restore signal path so proxy knows when session cache restore works.
- **LP-0MPNVNL0W008Z0L4** "Verify context cache behavior from live proxy logs" (completed, high) — Verified proxy-level session cache is working; llama checkpoint restore partially effective.
- **LP-0MPNTJ165004M5GX** "Investigate high CPU usage in llama-server" (completed, high) — Diagnosed root causes for high CPU usage in llama-server.
- **LP-0MQ5QVSMX007C5S4** "Improve proxy backend resilience: increase retries and adaptive timeouts" (completed, high) — Added adaptive timeout scaling by prompt token count.
- **LP-0MPHM16H9002O54V** "Add proxy timeouts and concurrency limits" (completed, high) — Added proxy timeouts and slot availability checks.

### Related Documents

- `proxy/README.md` — Proxy documentation; AC #4 requires adding target prompt size here.
- `proxy/proxy/prompt_resolver.py` — System prompt resolution with 64KB file size limit.
- `proxy/proxy/lifecycle.py` — Token estimation and adaptive timeout logic.
- `proxy/proxy/session_manager.py` — Session history tracking for delta ingestion.
- `proxy/prompts/assistant.txt` — Assistant system prompt file.
- `~/.pi/agent/prompts/` — Pi agent prompt templates (intake, plan, review, etc.) that contribute to prompt composition.
- `~/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md` — Core agent workflow instructions included in prompts.
- `~/.pi/agent/models.json` — Pi provider configuration showing proxy endpoint.
- `docs/dev/slot-thrashing-investigation/findings.md` — Previous slot thrashing investigation findings.

## Risks & Assumptions

- **Scope creep risk:** The investigation may uncover many potential optimizations. Mitigation: Record opportunities for additional features/refactorings as separate work items linked to LP-0MR5I2OK9007H5NU, rather than expanding this item's scope.
- **Agent framework access:** The pi agent framework (`~/.pi/agent/`) is on the same machine but its prompt construction logic may not be directly instrumentable. Mitigation: Work with available proxy logs, session files, and Pi session JSONL data.
- **Data availability:** The currently running proxy may not have retained detailed prompt body logs (system prompt content is redacted from log previews). Mitigation: Instrument the proxy to log per-role token counts without exposing sensitive content; use Pi session files for detailed prompt composition analysis.
- **Target size feasibility:** A target of 32K tokens (suggested in the work item) may or may not be achievable without breaking agent functionality. Mitigation: Recommendations should prioritize based on impact/effort and propose a realistic target.
- **Assumption:** The proxy is currently running and accessible at `http://192.168.0.199:8000` (confirmed via health check).
- **Assumption:** Pi session files in `~/.pi/agent/sessions/` contain the full request/response payloads needed for analysis.
- **Assumption:** The proxy's body logging redacts system prompts from log previews but the full body is forwarded to llama-server; instrumentation at the proxy can capture token counts per role category.

## Appendix: Clarifying Questions & Answers

- Q: "Where should the report on prompt composition live?" — Answer (user): Option b) As a work item comment on LP-0MR5I2OK9007H5NU. Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

- Q: "Does AC #3 mean just documenting recommendations or also implementing them?" — Answer (user): Recommendation only. The scope is documenting recommendations for reducing prompt size, not implementing them. Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

- Q: "What is the data source for investigation?" — Answer (user): The currently running proxy and any recent session in Pi. Source: interactive reply. Final: Investigation will use live proxy metrics/logs and pi session files from `~/.pi/agent/sessions/`.
data.title: Investigate and reduce agent prompt size inflation

data.description:
## Summary

The proxy currently accepts prompts of any size, including 93K-token prompts that take 19 minutes to process. There should be a configurable prompt size limit that rejects or truncates requests exceeding a reasonable threshold, preventing a single oversized request from blocking all other agents.

## User Story

As an operator running multiple agents through the proxy, I want the proxy to enforce a maximum prompt size so that one oversized request cannot monopolize the single available slot for 19 minutes.

## Current State

- No prompt size limit exists in the proxy
- Requests with 93K tokens are accepted and processed
- The single slot (`session_slot_pool_size: 1`) is occupied for the entire processing time
- All other agents are blocked until the oversized request completes or times out

## Desired Change

- Add a configurable `max_prompt_tokens` setting to `proxy/config.yaml` (default: 32768)
- When a request exceeds the limit, return an HTTP 400 error with a clear message indicating the prompt size and limit
- Log the rejection for monitoring
- Consider offering a `truncate` mode that automatically truncates prompts to the limit instead of rejecting

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `proxy/config.yaml` has a `max_prompt_tokens` setting (default: 32768)
2. Requests exceeding the limit are rejected with HTTP 400 and a clear error message
3. The rejection is logged with the request's prompt size and the configured limit
4. An optional `prompt_truncation` mode truncates oversized prompts instead of rejecting
5. Existing tests still pass
6. The proxy health endpoint remains responsive
data.title: Add prompt size limits to reject oversized requests

data.description:
# Fix fallback chain queuing: bypass queue on local slot busy, local-only concurrency=1, add slot release time logging

## Summary

Requests in a fallback chain are queued when local slots are busy instead of immediately falling through to the next provider. Local models lack a concurrency limit of 1, and console logging does not show when a slot will be released after a response completes.

## Problem Statement

When a model with a cascading fallback chain (e.g., the `plan` model: local-qwen3 → opencode-deepseek-free → opencode-go-deepseek) receives a request and all local slots are busy, the request is first queued by the JobScheduler before raising a 503 that triggers the fallback to remote providers. This queuing step adds unnecessary latency when fallback providers are available to serve the request immediately.

Additionally, there is no separate concurrency limit for local models — the global `max_concurrent_queries: 16` applies to all models equally, allowing more local requests than the single GPU can handle. Finally, operators cannot see from console logs when a busy slot is expected to become available again after a response completes.

## Users

- **AI agents (pi coding agents)** sending requests through the proxy to models with fallback chains (like `plan`). User story: "As an agent, when my request to the local model would be queued, I want it to immediately fall through to a remote provider instead of waiting in a queue that may never be served."
- **Operators/developers** debugging proxy behavior. User story: "As an operator, I want to see from the console logs when a slot will be released after a response completes, so I can estimate when queued requests will be served."
- **System administrators** managing proxy capacity. User story: "As an admin, I want local models to have a concurrency limit of 1 so a single GPU isn't overloaded, while remote models can handle many concurrent requests."

## Acceptance Criteria

1. **Queue bypass in fallback chains**: When a local model has fallback providers (remote providers or multiple providers) and all slots are busy, `proxy_with_fallback` in `provider.py` MUST skip the JobScheduler queue step entirely and immediately proceed to the next provider in the chain. The request MUST NOT be queued when fallback providers exist.

2. **Local model concurrency limit of 1**: A new configuration key `local_max_concurrent_queries` (default: 1) is added to `proxy/config.yaml` under `server:`. When processing a local provider request, the proxy limits concurrent local requests to this value. Remote providers continue to use the existing `max_concurrent_queries` (default: 16). If the local concurrency limit is reached, the request falls through to fallback providers immediately (as with AC1).

3. **Slot release time logging**: When a response is completed for a local model request that owns a JobScheduler slot, an INFO-level log message is emitted indicating when the slot's inactivity timeout will release it. Example: `"scheduler request complete slot=%s job=%s next_timeout_at=%.1fs"`. This helps operators estimate when queued sessions will be served.

4. **New tests verify queue bypass**: Unit tests in `test_provider_fallback.py` confirm that when a local model has fallback providers and all slots are busy, the request immediately falls through to the next provider without queuing.

5. **New tests verify local concurrency limit**: Unit tests confirm that when `local_max_concurrent_queries` is reached, the request falls through to fallback providers instead of being queued or rejected.

6. **All existing tests still pass** with the new changes.

7. **Full project test suite must pass** with the new changes.

8. **All related documentation is updated** to reflect the changes, including code comments, README, and any relevant wiki or docs site entries.

## Constraints

- Must not change the existing behavior for models without fallback providers (single local provider models like `qwen3`, `gemma4` continue to use direct `proxy_to_local`).
- The queue bypass (AC1) must only apply when `_has_fallback_providers()` returns `True` — i.e., when there are multiple providers or at least one remote provider.
- The local concurrency limit (AC2) must be configurable and default to 1. The existing `max_concurrent_queries` must continue to work for remote models.
- The slot release time logging (AC3) must not expose sensitive information. Use truncated session IDs (`session_id[:8]`) and slot IDs only.
- Must use the same logging patterns already established (`logger.info`, `session_id[:8]` truncation) for consistency.
- The existing `slot_job_timeout_seconds: 300.0` default must remain unchanged.

## Existing State

- **Slot scheduling**: The `JobScheduler` (in `slot_scheduler.py`) manages job-to-slot assignment with queueing. When all slots are busy, `admit_job` returns `QUEUED` (which in `router.py` raises `HTTPException(503)`). The `proxy_with_fallback` function in `provider.py` catches this exception and tries the next provider. The queue step is unnecessary when fallback providers exist.

- **Concurrency**: `proxy/config.yaml` has `max_concurrent_queries: 16` under `server:`. This is checked globally in `router.py`'s `proxy_to_local`. When the limit is reached, the code attempts remote fallback (if available) before returning a concurrency 503. There is no separate limit for local vs remote.

- **Slot timeout**: `slot_job_timeout_seconds: 300.0` is configured in the `slot_management` block of `config.yaml`. The `_periodic_timeout_check` task runs every 10 seconds and releases slots idle longer than the timeout.

- **Slot release logging**: When a slot is released via `release_slot` in `slot_scheduler.py`, an INFO log is emitted: `"scheduler release_slot slot=%s job=%s"`. However, there is no log indicating when the slot's inactivity timeout will fire (i.e., the projected release time). The `_check_timeouts_now` method releases slots that exceed `job_last_request_at + job_timeout`, but the projected timeout value is not logged.

- **Related work**: 
  - LP-0MR1F9SB3003SKU9 ("Pi retry cascade creates duplicate queries when fallback is slow") already added request coalescing to prevent retry cascades.
  - LP-0MQZS8HNS008VS1W ("Remote fallback returns 'All providers exhausted' instead of model_loading") fixed the local model not loaded case.
  - LP-0MQSRULFM006448I ("Add comprehensive logging to JobScheduler for slot lifecycle visibility") added scheduler logging for slot lifecycle events.

## Desired Change

1. **`proxy/proxy/router.py`** — In `proxy_to_local`, when the JobScheduler is active and would return `QUEUED` or `REJECTED_503` for a model with fallback providers, skip the queue step and instead signal to the caller to try the next provider.

2. **`proxy/proxy/provider.py`** — In `proxy_with_fallback`, when attempting a local provider, detect that a slot would be queued (not just exhausted or errored) and immediately proceed to the next provider without marking the local provider as unavailable.

3. **`proxy/proxy/slot_scheduler.py`** — In or after the request completion path (e.g., in `mark_request_end` or `release_slot`), add an INFO-level log message that includes the projected timeout time for the slot. The timeout is computed from `slot_state.job_last_request_at + self.job_timeout`.

4. **`proxy/config.yaml`** — Add `local_max_concurrent_queries: 1` under `server:`.

5. **`proxy/proxy/router.py`** — In `proxy_to_local`, implement separate concurrency tracking for local models. When `cur_local_active >= local_max_concurrent_queries`, immediately attempt fallback to remote providers without queuing.

6. **`proxy/tests/`** — Add unit tests for:
   - Queue bypass: model with fallback providers has local slot busy → immediately falls to next provider
   - Local concurrency limit: when `local_max_concurrent_queries` is exceeded, request falls through
   - Slot release time logging: verify log message contains timeout info

## Key Files

- `proxy/proxy/slot_scheduler.py` — JobScheduler class with `admit_job` returning QUEUED/REJECTED_503; `mark_request_end` needs timeout-projection logging
- `proxy/proxy/router.py` — `proxy_to_local` function that calls `admit_job` and raises HTTPException on QUEUED; also handles `max_concurrent_queries` globally
- `proxy/proxy/provider.py` — `proxy_with_fallback` function that catches exceptions from local providers and tries the next in the chain; needs queue-bypass awareness
- `proxy/config.yaml` — Configuration file; requires `local_max_concurrent_queries` addition
- `proxy/proxy/ui.py` — `_has_fallback_providers()` helper and `proxy_openai_api` routing code
- `proxy/tests/test_provider_fallback.py` — Existing fallback tests; needs new tests for queue bypass and concurrency changes
- `proxy/tests/test_slot_scheduler.py` — Existing scheduler tests; may need updates for timeout logging

## Related Work

| Item | Title | Status | Relationship |
|------|-------|--------|-------------|
| LP-0MR1F9SB3003SKU9 | Pi retry cascade creates duplicate queries when fallback is slow | in_review | Related: added request coalescing; reduced local_slot_exhaustion_retry_attempts |
| LP-0MQZS8HNS008VS1W | Remote fallback returns 'All providers exhausted' instead of model_loading | in_review | Related: fixed model-loading response preservation in fallback chains |
| LP-0MQSRULFM006448I | Add comprehensive logging to JobScheduler for slot lifecycle visibility | intake_complete | Related: added scheduler lifecycle logging (8 of 10 ACs implemented) |
| LP-0MQYEMXTT009OEVQ | Complete failover plan configuration with Pi instance integration | in_review | Related: created the `plan` fallback model chain that exhibits the queue-bypass issue |
| LP-0MQTG6EZ50028048 | JobScheduler premature slot release causing contention and 503 errors | completed | Related: fixed premature slot release; established active_requests tracking |
| LP-0MQ5FNQ9I006N4PO | Provider fallback for model requests | completed | Related: implemented the provider fallback mechanism |
| LP-0MQR0780Z006TLX6 | Improve slot management | in_review | Related: slot management redesign; coordinate queue-bypass changes |
| LP-0MQ0PYH8P008DLPJ | Web based logging per slot | open | Adjacent: web-based slot logging; this work adds console-level logging |

## Risks & Assumptions

- **Risk: Queue bypass may starve queued local requests.** If all local slots are busy and a new request with fallback providers immediately jumps to remote, local-queued sessions may wait longer. **Mitigation:** Queue bypass only applies when fallback providers exist. Sessions without fallback continue to queue normally.

- **Risk: Local concurrency=1 may cause false fallback during burst traffic.** A burst of requests all with the same session might trigger the concurrency limit incorrectly. **Mitigation:** Single-flight per-session already prevents duplicate in-flight requests for the same session (via request coalescing, LP-0MR1F9SB3003SKU9).

- **Risk: Interaction with slot management redesign (LP-0MQR0780Z006TLX6).** The ongoing slot management redesign may propose changes that overlap with this work's queue-bypass changes. **Mitigation:** Coordinate with LP-0MQR0780Z006TLX6 before implementing queue bypass.

- **Risk: Scope creep** — Additional improvements (dynamic provider ranking, health-based selection, per-provider concurrency limits) should be recorded as separate linked work items rather than expanding this item's scope.

- **Assumption:** The `QUEUED` result from `admit_job` can be intercepted before reaching the HTTPException raise in `router.py` to enable queue bypass.

- **Assumption:** The existing `active_queries` mechanism can be extended with a separate counter for local vs remote without significant refactoring.

- **Assumption:** The slot timeout projection log can be added at the response completion point (where `mark_request_end` is called or just before slot release) without surprising side effects.

## Appendix: Clarifying Questions & Answers

- **Q: "What is the current slot timeout?"** 
  - Answer (user): Keep the slot timeout at 300 seconds. Do not change the default.
  - Source: User feedback on intake draft. Final: confirmed.

- **Q: "What is the current concurrency limit for local models?"**
  - Answer (agent inference, code review): The global `max_concurrent_queries: 16` applies to all models. There is no separate local-model concurrency limit. The user wants local models limited to 1 concurrent request, while remote models keep the existing limit. Source: `proxy/config.yaml` line 212 and `proxy/proxy/router.py` line 270. Final: confirmed.

- **Q: "Does the current logging show when a slot will be released?"**
  - Answer (agent inference, code review): The `release_slot` method logs at INFO level `"scheduler release_slot slot=%s job=%s"`, but this fires at release time, not proactively. The timeout projection (`job_last_request_at + job_timeout`) is computed during `_check_timeouts_now` but only logged if the timeout has actually expired. There is no log that tells operators the projected timeout for active slots. The user wants a log message after response completion that indicates when the slot's inactivity timeout will fire. Source: `slot_scheduler.py` lines ~193, ~245-260. Final: confirmed.

- **Q: "Does the current fallback logging already show where a request is being sent?"**
  - Answer (agent inference, code review): The `proxy_with_fallback` function logs fallback events at INFO level. It does NOT log when a provider is first selected (only when fallback occurs), and the model field uses the request path rather than the resolved model name. Source: `proxy/proxy/provider.py`. Final: resolved by removing this from scope per user feedback (the current ACs focus on slot release time logging instead).

data.title: Documentation: findings report and README update

data.description:
## Problem

When a request is dispatched to the local llama-server via `proxy_to_local`, the server may internally queue it even when its single slot (`--parallel 1`) is busy. This happens because:

1. `proxy_to_local` only checks `max_concurrent_queries: 16` (global limit), not `local_max_concurrent_queries: 1`
2. `_check_slot_availability` is unreliable in router mode — the `/slots` endpoint may return an empty list or non-list response, causing `total_slots=0` and silently passing the check
3. There is no authoritative gate at the proxy level that says "local is busy, do not send"

The result: llama-server queues requests internally, consuming resources and hiding the slot-busy condition from the proxy's fallback logic.

## Goal

Make the proxy the authoritative dispatcher — it tracks which session currently owns the local backend and refuses to dispatch a second local request until the first completes. Session IDs are used as the unique key into the tracking state.

## Proposed Design

### 1. Local dispatch tracker (`proxy/dispatch.py`)

```python
# session_id -> {backend: "local"|"remote", started_at: float}
active_dispatches: dict[str, dict] = {}
active_dispatches_lock = asyncio.Lock()
```

Provides:
- `try_acquire_local(session_id) -> bool` — returns False if local already busy
- `release_local(session_id)` — releases the local slot
- `local_owner() -> Optional[str]` — which session holds the local slot

### 2. Enforce in `proxy_to_local` (router.py)

Add a check at the top of `proxy_to_local`:
- If `local_active_queries >= local_max_concurrent_queries`, return 503 immediately
- This prevents the request from ever reaching llama-server when local is busy

### 3. Enforce in `_do_proxy_openai_api` (ui.py)

Before the grace window or when routing to local, check if local is busy:
- If busy and the request has fallback providers → route to remote
- If busy and no fallback → return 503 with Retry-After

### 4. Session tracking for observability

Maintain a `dict[session_id, backend_target]` for active requests so logs and status endpoints can report which session currently owns the local slot.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. When a local request is in-flight, a second request to ANY model on the same local backend gets 503 (or remote fallback) — NOT silently queued by llama-server
2. The session_id of the current local owner is visible via status/debug endpoints
3. Existing session single-flight (per-session dedup) remains intact
4. All existing tests pass
5. New tests verify the local concurrency gate
data.title: Proxy-level dispatch tracker: enforce single local request with session tracking

data.description:
# Refactor Assessment Epic (2026-07)

## Summary

This epic tracks **behavior-preserving refactoring opportunities** identified from a maintainability assessment of the proxy runtime codebase.

Assessment scope:
- Product/docs context: `README.md`, `LLAMA_README.md`, `docs/llama-router.md`, `proxy/README.md`
- Code focus: `proxy/proxy/*.py`
- Test focus: `proxy/tests/`

## Product context

The proxy is an OpenAI-compatible gateway that routes requests between local llama-server and remote providers, with session-aware incremental ingestion, slot/scheduler coordination, provider fallback, model lifecycle management, and web/admin observability endpoints.

## Why this epic exists

The external behavior is already broad and well-tested, but several internals remain expensive to evolve safely due to very large orchestration functions and duplicated control-flow branches. This epic captures incremental refactors that reduce complexity while preserving behavior.

## Refactor protocol result

### Phase 1 — Assess

1. Understood behavior from docs and high-level files.
2. Identified code smells primarily in these hotspots:
   - long methods / large orchestration functions
   - duplicated fallback logic
   - duplicated local-model loading branches
   - duplicate status-shaping/parsing logic
3. Reviewed test landscape:
   - Extensive suite exists (unit + integration + Playwright).
   - Strong coverage around routing/fallback/status behaviors.
   - Additional targeted tests are still needed per refactor slice to lock behavior contracts locally.

### Phase 2 — Plan

Children under this epic are concrete, independently deliverable refactor opportunities with location, technique, and behavior-locking tests.

## Priority scale used in children

- Refactor priority `1`: critical maintainability bottleneck (mapped to `high` Worklog priority)
- Refactor priority `2`: high-impact simplification (mapped to `medium` Worklog priority)
- Refactor priority `3`: lower-impact cleanup (mapped to `low` Worklog priority)

## Acceptance Criteria

- Child work items exist for each approved refactor opportunity.
- Each child includes:
  - exact location (file/function)
  - smell and impact
  - refactoring technique
  - tests proving behavior remains unchanged
- No feature work is bundled with these refactors.

## Idempotence note

This epic uses stable `REFACTOR:` child titles so reruns can detect and reuse existing items instead of creating duplicates.
data.title: REFACTOR: 2026-07 maintainability backlog for proxy runtime

data.description:
## Refactor Priority
- Refactor priority: `1` (critical maintainability bottleneck)
- Worklog priority mapping: `high`

## Location
- `proxy/proxy/router.py`
  - `proxy_to_local()` (~lines 170-1316; ~1147 LOC)
  - nested `stream_generator()` (~lines 683-1044)

## Code smell
`proxy_to_local()` mixes too many responsibilities in one function:
- request/session normalization
- scheduler admission and slot restore/save
- streaming and non-streaming request execution
- guardrail/token-rate handling
- disconnect cleanup and counters
- response header shaping

This increases cognitive load, makes regression root-cause analysis slow, and creates repeated cleanup/session-update branches between streaming and buffered paths.

## Why this is a problem
Any small behavior adjustment in one path risks divergence from the other path due to duplicated or interleaved logic. The function is currently a maintenance bottleneck for reliability fixes.

## Refactoring technique
- **Extract Method** for major phases into pure helpers
- **Template Method / Pipeline extraction** for shared post-response/session-update steps
- **Introduce small data object** for per-request execution context (session, slot, counters)

## Behavior-preserving test plan
Existing tests to keep green:
- `proxy/tests/test_backend_resilience.py`
- `proxy/tests/test_disconnect_detection.py`
- `proxy/tests/test_local_dispatch_gate.py`
- `proxy/tests/test_token_rate_integration.py`

Add/strengthen targeted parity tests:
1. Same session header outputs for stream and non-stream paths (`X-Session-*`).
2. Slot save/restore and scheduler mark_request_start/end parity across both paths.
3. Identical disconnect cleanup semantics for streaming generator close/error paths.
4. Identical guardrail cutoff/invalidation behavior before vs after extraction.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `proxy_to_local()` becomes an orchestration entrypoint with extracted helpers (smaller and phase-oriented).
- Shared logic between streaming and non-streaming branches is deduplicated without behavior drift.
- Existing tests above remain passing.
- New parity tests demonstrate unchanged HTTP status, headers, and session/slot side effects.

## Rationale
This refactor reduces blast radius for future fixes in routing/session logic while preserving all external API behavior.
data.title: REFACTOR: Decompose local routing pipeline in proxy_to_local

data.description:
Unify duplicated provider fallback state machines in `proxy_with_remote_fallback()` and `proxy_with_fallback()` into shared, extracted primitives — preserving all external behaviour, selection order, cooldown semantics, and response payload shape.

## Refactor Priority
- Refactor priority: `1` (critical maintainability bottleneck)
- Worklog priority mapping: `high`

## Location
- `proxy/proxy/provider.py`
  - `proxy_with_remote_fallback()` (~lines 591-808; ~218 LOC)
  - `proxy_with_fallback()` (~lines 811-1422; ~612 LOC)

## Code smell
Large duplicated fallback state-machine logic appears in both functions:
- provider iteration and exclusion/cooldown
- HTTP error classification
- empty-response handling
- attempts diagnostics and exhausted response shaping
- repeated fallback logging branches

## Why this is a problem
Duplicated decision trees make behavior drift likely (one path fixed while the other path misses the fix), especially for edge conditions like `model_loading`, slot exhaustion, and local lease contention.

## Refactoring technique
- **Extract Method** for reusable fallback decision primitives
- **Strategy extraction** for local-vs-remote provider differences
- **Consolidate Duplicate Conditional Fragments** for attempts/cooldown bookkeeping

## Behavior-preserving test plan
Existing tests to keep green:
- `proxy/tests/test_provider_fallback.py`
- `proxy/tests/test_ui_transparent_lease_fallback.py`
- `proxy/tests/test_prompt_injection_integration.py`

Add/strengthen targeted parity tests:
1. Matrix test for status/error classes: `429`, `503 model_loading`, `5xx`, empty `200`.
2. Ensure unchanged `X-Provider` header behavior for successful provider selection.
3. Ensure unchanged exhausted-response payload semantics (including first-error preservation rules).
4. Ensure unchanged cooldown handling for local `4xx` vs remote errors.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Shared fallback primitives are extracted and used by both fallback entrypoints.
- No externally observable change in selection order, cooldown semantics, or response payload shape.
- Existing provider fallback tests continue to pass.
- Additional parity tests cover previously duplicated branches.
- All related documentation is updated to reflect the changes, including code comments, README, and any relevant wiki or docs site entries.
- Full project test suite must pass with the new changes.

## Rationale
Centralizing fallback logic lowers maintenance cost and reduces subtle divergence risk in one of the proxy's most critical reliability paths.

data.description:
## Refactor Priority
- Refactor priority: `2` (high-impact simplification)
- Worklog priority mapping: `medium`

## Location
- `proxy/proxy/ui.py`
  - `create_embeddings()` (~lines 541-750)
  - `_do_proxy_openai_api()` (~lines 777-1000)

## Code smell
These handlers contain near-duplicate local-model orchestration logic:
- resolve/validate target local model
- fast router loaded-check path
- background load scheduling
- session slot restore detection gating model-loading response
- remote-provider fallback before returning model-loading

## Why this is a problem
Behavioral fixes must be patched in two long functions, increasing drift risk between embeddings and chat/completions pathways.

## Refactoring technique
- **Extract Method** into a shared orchestration helper for local model dispatch readiness
- **Parameterize by endpoint** (`v1/embeddings` vs `v1/<path>`) while reusing the same decision flow

## Behavior-preserving test plan
Existing tests to keep green:
- `proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_router_unit.py`
- `proxy/tests/test_provider_fallback.py`
- `proxy/tests/test_prompt_injection_integration.py`
- `proxy/tests/test_alias_plan_code_integration.py`

Add/strengthen targeted parity tests:
1. Equivalent model-loading response shape between embeddings and chat path when model not ready.
2. Equivalent remote-fallback-before-model-loading behavior.
3. Equivalent scheduler reenter + slot-file heuristics for session restore detection.
4. Equivalent success path when router reports model already loaded.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Shared local-model orchestration helper used by both handlers.
- Endpoint-specific behavior (request body validation for embeddings, prompt composition for chat) remains unchanged.
- Existing tests remain passing.
- New parity tests confirm no semantic drift between handler pathways.

## Rationale
This refactor reduces duplication in a high-churn area where model-loading and fallback behavior evolves frequently.
data.title: REFACTOR: Extract shared local-model load orchestration from UI handlers

data.description:
## Refactor Priority
- Refactor priority: `2` (high-impact simplification)
- Worklog priority mapping: `medium`

## Location
- `proxy/proxy/handlers.py`
  - `get_llama_local_status()` (~lines 186-347)
- `proxy/proxy/observability.py`
  - `query_llama_status()` (~lines 227-400)

## Code smell
Status collection, endpoint probing/parsing, and response shaping are split across multiple large functions with partially overlapping responsibilities.

Observed issues:
- repeated defensive parsing branches (`response.json()` / `response.text` / dict/list normalization)
- response-shaping concerns mixed with network querying concerns
- similar timeout/fallback behavior encoded in multiple places

## Why this is a problem
Status endpoints are operationally critical. Duplicated parsing and shaping logic increases the chance of inconsistent fields or subtle regressions during observability changes.

## Refactoring technique
- **Extract Method** for reusable HTTP JSON parsing helpers
- **Introduce Facade / Query service** for llama status data gathering
- **Separate query from presentation** (status snapshot builder vs endpoint response formatting)

## Behavior-preserving test plan
Existing tests to keep green:
- `proxy/tests/test_query_llama_status.py`
- `proxy/tests/test_status_integration.py`
- `proxy/tests/test_llama_local_status.py`
- `proxy/tests/test_observability_regression.py`

Add/strengthen targeted parity tests:
1. Stable field schema and defaults for `/llama/local/status` under timeout/error conditions.
2. Stable router-mode detection behavior from `/props` fallback.
3. Stable handling for mocked async/sync `response.json()` behavior variants.
4. Stable latency guard behavior for status endpoint timeout path.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Common status query/parsing helpers extracted and reused.
- `get_llama_local_status()` and `query_llama_status()` responsibilities are clearly separated.
- Existing status/observability tests remain passing.
- Added parity tests lock response schema and default-value behavior.

## Rationale
Improves reliability of operational status reporting while reducing maintenance overhead for future observability work.
data.title: REFACTOR: Consolidate llama-status collection and response shaping

data.description:
## Refactor Priority
- Refactor priority: `3` (lower-impact maintainability cleanup)
- Worklog priority mapping: `low`

## Location
- `proxy/proxy/server.py`
  - `lifespan()` (~lines 244-438)

## Code smell
`lifespan()` currently combines many startup/shutdown concerns:
- config/logger/bootstrap setup
- version capture tasks
- podman migration side-effect block
- background default-model loading loop
- watchdog/task wiring
- persistence/cleanup task orchestration
- shutdown teardown

## Why this is a problem
The function is difficult to reason about and test in isolation, especially around startup failure modes and side effects. Small changes in startup policy can unintentionally affect unrelated teardown behavior.

## Refactoring technique
- **Extract Method** into explicit startup/shutdown phases
- **Guard Clause simplification** for side-effectful setup blocks
- **Introduce narrow helpers** for task lifecycle management (create/cancel/await)

## Behavior-preserving test plan
Existing tests to keep green:
- `proxy/tests/test_lifecycle_host_start.py`
- `proxy/tests/test_model_lifecycle_router_unit.py`
- `proxy/tests/test_backend_resilience.py`

Add/strengthen targeted parity tests:
1. Startup initializes equivalent background tasks and flags.
2. Shutdown cancels/awaits the same tasks and clears client/process state consistently.
3. Podman-migrate failure path still logs warnings and does not abort startup.
4. Default model background-load retry schedule remains unchanged.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `lifespan()` reduced to high-level orchestration with extracted helpers.
- Startup/shutdown behavior remains identical in logs, task creation, and state toggles.
- Existing lifecycle/resilience tests remain passing.
- Added parity tests cover startup/shutdown side effects explicitly.

## Rationale
Improves readability and testability of service lifecycle code with minimal behavioral risk.
data.title: REFACTOR: Split FastAPI lifespan startup/shutdown orchestration

data.description:
# Session Recording: capture raw client-proxy-provider message payloads to disk for debugging and audit

## Problem statement

When debugging or auditing proxy behavior, operators currently have no mechanism to review the complete message payloads exchanged during a session — including prompts sent by the client, the processed payload forwarded to the provider, and the final assembled response returned to the client. Existing request logging (LP-0MQQSM1V7004QOGL) captures only a body preview and session/slot metadata. A full session recording feature is needed to enable post-hoc review of all message traffic for debugging, auditing, and analysis purposes.

## Users

- **Operators and SREs** running the proxy: need the ability to review the complete request-response history of any session to diagnose issues (e.g., prompt injection, unexpected model behavior, context corruption).
- **Developers** building and testing proxy features: need session recordings to verify correct prompt transformation, delta handling, and response assembly.
- **AI agents (Ralph/Map)** working through the proxy: session recordings provide an auditable trail of what was sent and received.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. All proxy sessions are recorded to disk, capturing only the **message payloads** (prompt messages and completion/embedding responses — not full HTTP headers) for both the incoming client request and the final assembled response from the provider.
2. For streaming responses, the individual SSE chunks are fully assembled into a single final response before being written to disk.
3. Recordings include the client→proxy request payload (the messages/prompt sent by the client), the proxy→provider request payload (after any proxy processing/transformation), and the provider→client response payload (the final assembled completion/embeddings result).
4. Recordings are organized on disk by session ID with timestamps (e.g., a directory structure such as `<recording-path>/<session-id>/<timestamp>-request.json` and `<recording-path>/<session-id>/<timestamp>-response.json`).
5. The recording path is configurable via `proxy/config.yaml` (defaulting to `proxy/session-recordings/`).
6. Recording is **always-on** (no per-session or per-request toggle) and introduces minimal performance overhead (non-blocking file writes).
7. Recording covers both local routing (`proxy_to_local`) and remote routing (`proxy_to_remote`).
8. A helper endpoint or command is available to list and retrieve session recordings (e.g., `GET /admin/sessions/<session-id>/recordings`).
9. **New child work items are created under the existing cleanup epic (LP-0MQW94K86004CJ59)** to handle retention and cleanup of session recording files (e.g., retention policy, disk space limits, integration with cleanup scripts).
10. **Full project test suite must pass with the new changes.**
11. **All related documentation is updated to reflect the changes, including code comments, README, and any relevant wiki or docs site entries.**

## Constraints

- Only message payloads are recorded — not full HTTP headers, system prompt content, or internal routing metadata (unless explicitly needed for audit context).
- Streaming responses must be fully assembled before writing to disk (do not record individual SSE chunks).
- Recording must be non-blocking: use background tasks or file I/O off the hot path (e.g., `asyncio.create_task` for file writes).
- Recordings must not contain sensitive data beyond what the client and provider already exchanged (no proxy-internal secrets or API keys).
- Storage format: JSON files per request-response pair, organized by session ID.
- Must not significantly increase request latency; file writes should be off the critical path.

## Existing state

- The proxy has existing request logging via `log_request()` in `proxy/router_helpers.py` which logs method, URL, body preview (500 chars, system messages stripped), session ID, slot ID, and session headers at INFO level.
- Session management is handled by `session_manager.py` with session state stored in memory and optionally persisted.
- The proxy routes requests through either `proxy_to_local` (router.py) → local llama-server or `proxy_to_remote` (proxy_remote.py) → remote API.
- Streaming responses are assembled from SSE chunks in both local and remote paths before forwarding to the client.
- The existing cleanup epic (LP-0MQW94K86004CJ59 — Pi disk space management: session logs, model caches, and KV cache cleanup) covers cleanup of pi agent session logs, model caches, and container images. Session recording cleanup will be added as a child of this epic.
- There is no current mechanism for persistent session recording to disk.

## Risks & assumptions

- **Risk: Disk space exhaustion from accumulated recordings.** Session recordings persist on disk indefinitely without a retention policy. *Mitigation: cleanup and retention will be added as child work items under LP-0MQW94K86004CJ59 (cleanup epic). Default retention policy must be defined during implementation.*
- **Risk: Recording increases request latency.** Synchronous file writes on the critical path could slow down proxy response times. *Mitigation: use non-blocking I/O (asyncio background tasks) for all recording writes; recording must never block request processing.*
- **Risk: Sensitive data exposure in recordings.** Recordings contain user prompts and model responses, which may include PII or sensitive business data. *Mitigation: recordings are stored on the local filesystem only; no automatic sharing or network transmission. Document storage security expectations in README.*
- **Risk: Scope creep into additional features** (e.g., replay, search, export, real-time monitoring). *Mitigation: record opportunities for additional features as separate linked work items rather than expanding this item's scope.*
- **Assumption:** The assembled response is available at the point of recording in both streaming and non-streaming paths (confirmed by reading proxy_to_local and proxy_to_remote — both assemble the final response before returning to the caller).
- **Assumption:** Session IDs are stable and unique identifiers that can be used as directory keys (confirmed: SessionManager generates unique IDs).
- **Assumption:** The recording directory will be on a filesystem with sufficient space; no separate volume or mount is required.

## Desired change

- Add a new module `proxy/session_recorder.py` (or similar) that provides functions to record session request-response pairs to disk.
- Modify `proxy_to_local` and `proxy_to_remote` to invoke the session recorder with the assembled request payload and final response payload, keyed by session ID.
- For streaming responses, capture the assembled response after all chunks have been received before returning it to the client.
- Add configuration option in `proxy/config.yaml` for the recording path and any other settings.
- Add an admin endpoint to list/retrieve session recordings.
- Under the existing cleanup epic (LP-0MQW94K86004CJ59), create child work items for: retention policy definition, a cleanup script for session recordings, and cron integration.

**Key Files:**

- `proxy/proxy/session_recorder.py` — New module implementing session recording logic (request/response capture, file I/O, directory organization).
- `proxy/proxy/router.py` — Modify `proxy_to_local` to invoke session recorder after response assembly.
- `proxy/proxy/proxy_remote.py` — Modify `proxy_to_remote` to invoke session recorder after response assembly.
- `proxy/proxy/ui.py` — Add admin endpoint for listing/retrieving session recordings.
- `proxy/config.yaml` — Add `session_recording` configuration section with path and options.
- `proxy/proxy/server.py` — Register the admin endpoint routes; import and initialize session recorder.
- `scripts/cleanup-session-recordings.sh` — New cleanup script for session recording retention (child of LP-0MQW94K86004CJ59 epic).
- `tests/test_session_recorder.py` — Unit tests for the recording module.
- `tests/test_cleanup_session_recordings.sh` — Tests for the cleanup script (child of LP-0MQW94K86004CJ59 epic).

## Related work

Potentially related docs:
- `proxy/proxy/router_helpers.py` — Contains `log_request()` which is the existing request logging function.
- `proxy/proxy/router.py` — Contains `proxy_to_local()` where recording hook would be added for local routing.
- `proxy/proxy/proxy_remote.py` — Contains `proxy_to_remote()` where recording hook would be added for remote routing.
- `proxy/proxy/session_manager.py` — Session management and state; relevant context for session keying.
- `proxy/config.yaml` — Configuration file where recording path would be added.

Potentially related work items:
- **LP-0MQW94K86004CJ59** — Pi disk space management: session logs, model caches, and KV cache cleanup (epic, plan_complete). This epic will be extended with child items for session recording cleanup.
- **LP-0MR4XEDEY009JGVC** — Implement: pi session log retention script (blocked). Child of the cleanup epic; session recording cleanup will follow a similar pattern.
- **LP-0MQQSM1V7004QOGL** — Log request info in proxy (completed). Enhanced request-level logging — precedent for observability additions without breaking changes.
- **LP-0MO6EWJ5K0088CBS** — Session ID generation and management core (completed). Implements the SessionManager that generates and manages session IDs — recordings will be keyed on these session IDs.

## Appendix: Clarifying questions & answers

- Q: "What does 'raw content' include? Should we capture the full HTTP request/response (headers + body) for both the client→proxy and proxy→provider paths, or just the message payloads (prompts, completions)? And should streaming responses be recorded as individual chunks or as the assembled final response?" — Answer (user): "Message payloads only, streamed content as assembled final response". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

- Q: "When should recording be active? Should this be always-on, or configurable (e.g., enable per-session, per-request, or globally)?" — Answer (user): "Always-on". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

- Q: "How does this relate to the existing cleanup epic (LP-0MQW94K86004CJ59 — Pi disk space management)? Your description says to 'add cleanup requirements to the work item we have on disk/log/memory management.' Should the recording feature's retention/cleanup be integrated into that existing epic's scope, or should this be a standalone work item with its own cleanup documentation?" — Answer (user): "a (add it to the ACs for the epic)". Source: interactive reply. Final: yes.

## Summary

Comprehensive test suite covering the session recorder module, integration hooks, streaming response assembly, admin endpoint, and configuration. Defines the validation contract that all implementation features must satisfy (test-first approach).

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Tests cover request/response payload capture for both streaming and non-streaming paths
2. Tests verify assembled (not chunk-wise) recording for streaming responses
3. Tests cover directory creation, file naming (by session ID + timestamp), and file I/O edge cases (permissions, disk full)
4. Tests verify non-blocking behavior (recording does not delay response)
5. Tests cover both proxy_to_local and proxy_to_remote recording hooks
6. Tests cover admin endpoint listing and retrieval of recordings
7. Tests cover config override and default values
8. Recorder handles missing recording directory by creating it (negative case)
9. All tests pass with the existing proxy test suite

## Minimal Implementation

1. Create `tests/test_session_recorder.py` with unit tests for the session_recorder module
2. Create integration tests for recording hooks in both routing paths
3. Create tests for the admin endpoint
4. Use mocking to verify non-blocking behavior
5. Test edge cases: streaming vs non-streaming, empty payloads, disk errors, missing directories

## Deliverables

- `tests/test_session_recorder.py`
- `tests/test_admin_recordings.py`

## Dependencies

- None (test-first: defines the contract)

data.description:
## Summary

Create the proxy/session_recorder.py module with all core recording logic (request/response capture, directory organization, non-blocking file I/O) and add the session_recording configuration section to proxy/config.yaml.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. record_request(session_id, direction, payload) captures a request payload to disk as a JSON file under <recording-path>/<session-id>/<timestamp>-request.json
2. record_response(session_id, direction, payload) captures the assembled response as <recording-path>/<session-id>/<timestamp>-response.json
3. Three directions are captured: client_to_proxy, proxy_to_provider, provider_to_client
4. Streaming responses are assembled before being passed to the recorder (recorder accepts only assembled payloads)
5. File writes are non-blocking (asyncio.create_task or equivalent background I/O)
6. Recording path defaults to proxy/session-recordings/ and is configurable via config.yaml
7. Directory structure is created automatically if it does not exist
8. Existing tests pass, new tests from the test suite pass

## Minimal Implementation

1. Create proxy/session_recorder.py module with AsyncSessionRecorder class
2. Implement record_request() and record_response() methods with JSON serialization
3. Use asyncio.create_task for non-blocking file writes
4. Organize files as <recording-path>/<session-id>/<timestamp>-{request,response}.json
5. Add session_recording config section to proxy/config.yaml with path option

## Deliverables

- proxy/proxy/session_recorder.py
- proxy/config.yaml update

## Dependencies

- Blocked-by: LP-0MR8FPNDJ008JRNY (Test suite for session recording — test-first)

## Parent

LP-0MR8FEKK6005V9ML
